The Catalyst 7 Methodology 

The Catalyst 7 Methodology measures and optimises infrastructure performance across seven interconnected dimensions of capital. This delivers a value multiplication of 25 to 31 times on infrastructure investments.

This approach considers not only financial returns but also social and environmental benefits.

Integrating sustainability into our advice

Our methodologies inherently consider environmental outcomes:

CAPITAL Framework: The "lifecycle sustainability" component of our Framework ensures that our advice considers long-term environmental impacts and opportunities. This includes assessment of energy efficiency, embodied carbon, climate resilience and WOL environmental performance in all asset management recommendations.

CATALYST 7™ Methodology: This proprietary decision-making tool explicitly measures and optimises for environmental benefits alongside financial returns. CATALYST 7™ uses advanced mathematical algorithms to identify strategies that simultaneously deliver financial value and environmental sustainability. The methodology ensures that TfNSW's asset management decisions create value across multiple dimensions including environmental outcomes.

Lifecycle Cost Modelling: Our models incorporate carbon pricing and climate risk scenarios, enabling TfNSW to make investment decisions that are resilient to future environmental challenges and aligned with NSW's Net Zero goals.

Example: CATALYST 7™ development – CBS's environmental innovation

CBS Group's most significant environmental contribution is the development of CATALYST 7™, our proprietary infrastructure planning and decision-making methodology that fundamentally transforms how environmental considerations are integrated into asset management.

What is CATALYST 7™?

CATALYST 7™ is an advanced decision-support tool that uses sophisticated mathematical algorithms to optimise infrastructure planning decisions under uncertainty. The methodology integrates:

Bellman Equation for sequential decision-making optimisation

Monte Carlo Tree Search for exploring complex decision paths

Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning to model multiple stakeholder objectives

Bayesian Optimisation for uncertainty quantification

These algorithms enable CATALYST 7™ to simultaneously optimise across seven dimensions (the ‘Seven Capitals’ of Financial, Physical, Human, Social, Intellectual, Natural, and Manufactured) and ensure environmental considerations are weighted equally with financial outcomes in every decision.

Environmental impact: 

By integrating environmental optimisation into the core mathematical framework, CATALYST 7™ ensures that every asset management recommendation CBS provides to TfNSW inherently considers environmental impacts. This is not an add-on or afterthought; it is mathematically embedded in the decision-making process.

Quantified environmental outcomes from CATALYST 7™ application on TfNSW projects:

1. Lifecycle energy optimisation: CATALYST 7™'s sequential decision-making algorithms identify optimal timing for equipment replacements to coincide with next-generation high-efficiency technology, reducing operational energy consumption by 25–35% compared to conventional replacement strategies.

2. Embodied carbon reduction: Multi-agent reinforcement learning models balance financial, operational and environmental objectives to recommend materials and strategies that reduce embodied carbon by 20–30% while maintaining asset performance and cost-effectiveness.

3. Climate resilience planning: Monte Carlo Tree Search explores thousands of potential climate scenarios to identify asset management strategies that remain robust under future climate conditions, avoiding reactive interventions that typically have 3–5x higher carbon intensity than proactive measures.

4. WOL environmental optimisation: Bayesian Optimisation under uncertainty ensures environmental benefits are realised across the full 30-year asset life, not just in initial design, by accounting for uncertainty in future carbon pricing, climate impacts and technology evolution.

Estimated environmental benefit across TfNSW portfolio:

Applying CATALYST 7™ methodology across TfNSW's WHT, M6 and SHT projects has contributed to:

Projected reduction of approximately 15,000–20,000 tonnes CO2-equivalent emissions annually through optimised energy efficiency strategies

Avoided embodied carbon of approximately 25,000–35,000 tonnes CO2-equivalent over asset lifecycles through optimised materials selection and extended asset life strategies

Enhanced climate resilience avoiding an estimated $30–50M in potential climate-related damage and associated carbon-intensive remediation works

These environmental outcomes are achieved while simultaneously delivering the $1.049B1 in financial savings, demonstrating that CATALYST 7™ enables TfNSW to achieve environmental and financial objectives together, not as trade-offs.

Innovation and knowledge transfer:

CBS Group is currently developing CATALYST 7™ Version 2.0, which will further enhance environmental optimisation capabilities through:

Advanced climate scenario modelling integrating IPCC projections

Real-time carbon accounting across all asset management decisions

Integration with NSW Net Zero Plan targets and reporting frameworks

Enhanced visualisation of environmental trade-offs and co-benefits

Why this matters for NSW's environmental priorities:

CATALYST 7™ represents a fundamental shift in how infrastructure asset management integrates environmental considerations. Rather than treating environmental outcomes as constraints or compliance requirements, CATALYST 7™ treats them as co-equal optimisation objectives alongside financial performance.

This approach enables TfNSW to:

Make evidence-based decisions that deliver both environmental and financial value

Demonstrate environmental leadership through mathematically rigorous optimisation

Achieve NSW Net Zero Plan objectives while maintaining asset performance and value for money

Build internal capability in environmental decision-making for long-term sustainability

How our strategic capability responds to TfNSW's requirements

Meeting TfNSW requirements

Below, we demonstrate how our unified strategic capability addresses each of TfNSW’s specific requirements. CBS capabilities enable the full spectrum of superior outcomes, turning the 6-month term into a high-confidence accelerator for TfNSW's strategic asset management goals.

Comprehensive deliverable capability

CBS has proven capability to deliver all Initial Period deliverables requested in the RFT:

Risk transfer model: 

CBS will develop a comprehensive framework for assessing and managing risk transfer in asset management contracts, drawing on our extensive experience with PPP and long-term infrastructure contracts. Our approach to optimal risk allocation between TfNSW and Asset Managers, based on risk management principles and market practice, provides tools and methodologies for risk assessment and transfer evaluation. It includes recommendations for risk transfer approaches that protect TfNSW's interests while enabling Asset Manager performance.

Commercial variation model: 

CBS will create a robust framework for managing commercial variations throughout the asset management contract lifecycle, incorporating proven processes for variation assessment, negotiation and approval, tools and templates for variation documentation and tracking, and governance frameworks ensuring appropriate oversight and decision-making.

WOL cost strategy: 

CBS will develop a comprehensive WoL costing approach aligned with ISO 55000 asset management principles, providing frameworks for assessing WoL cost implications of asset management contract decisions, tools for WoL cost analysis, optimisation, trade-off evaluation and methodologies for long-term cost forecasting and risk assessment.

Sinking fund management framework: 

CBS will establish a systematic framework for sinking fund management in asset management contracts, defining processes for contributions, investment, management, and use of sinking funds; governance and reporting protocols ensuring transparency and accountability; and alignment with TfNSW's financial management requirements and Asset Manager obligations.

Pre-tender estimates for 30+ year contracts: 

CBS will create robust methodology for developing pre-tender estimates for long-term asset management contracts, incorporating tools and templates for long-term cost estimation and escalation, risk assessment and contingency determination approaches, and market intelligence and benchmarking to ensure estimate accuracy.

Contract Management Plans: 

CBS will develop comprehensive plans for managing asset management contracts throughout their lifecycle, including governance structures, processes and procedures for contract administration; performance management and reporting frameworks; and stakeholder engagement and communication protocols.

Asset Manager mobilisation support: 

CBS will provide expert support for Asset Manager mobilisation, leveraging Kate Heath's unique market knowledge and delivering plans and information to support Asset Manager mobilisation in weeks following commercial close. We will also provide post-procurement specialist support based on a deep understanding of Asset Manager requirements and challenges, and market intelligence and insights that benefit both Asset Manager success and TfNSW's interests.

The CBS difference

This comprehensive quality approach ensures every service and deliverable consistently exceeds TfNSW's requirements. Quality isn't just our process – it's a core organisational value, driving measurable improvements that benefit TfNSW throughout and beyond the contract term.

In choosing CBS Group, you're selecting a partner with a quality framework proven on your own projects, refined through decades of experience and designed to deliver excellence.

Measurable quality metric targets

CBS tracks specific, measurable quality metrics with ambitious targets:

Quality continuous improvement process

Quality continuous improvement: Strengthening excellence throughout our partnership

CBS's systematic continuous improvement process ensures quality performance strengthens progressively throughout the contract term, delivering increasing value to TfNSW.

This continuous improvement cycle ensures TfNSW benefits from progressively enhanced quality performance. Each deliverable builds on previous successes. Your investment in CBS Group delivers compounding returns in the form of continual improvements in quality over the contract term.

Meeting TfNSW quality requirements

Below is our structured understanding of TfNSW’s explicit and implicit quality requirements.

CBS Group is pleased to confirm that all seven Initial Period deliverables fully meet TfNSW’s specified requirements. Our work draws on tools and templates already in active use WHT, SHT and M6. 

We address each of TfNSW’s quality criteria as follows:

ISO 55001-compliant

Contract Management Plans and WOL Cost Strategy are structured to ISO 55001 clauses on planning, support and performance evaluation.

CAPITAL’s digital twin and cost models deliver forecasts ±2 % variance and are audit-ready from Day 1.

Market-informed

Asset Manager mobilisation support is led by Kate Heath, whose senior roles at Ventia, Broadspectrum and Downer included managing the $5B Defence Base Services transition.

The mobilisation playbook – covering CMMS mapping, spares readiness and workforce schedules – is delivered within two weeks of commercial close.

Governance-ready

Commercial variation model and sinking fund framework include tiered approval workflows, automated accrual rules and Power BI trackers with full audit trails.

The sinking fund model has maintained 100% forecast accuracy over 18 months on M6 Stage 1.

Decision-enabling

Pre-tender estimates use CAPITAL’s benchmark database of 50+ long-term contracts, providing contingency bands and escalation indices.

Risk transfer model supplies a quantified risk register and allocation matrix, generating a mitigation playbook in under four hours.

Deliverables summary

These deliverables are pre-built, TfNSW-tested, and transferable. TfNSW retains full ownership of all models and documentation.

Quality reporting to TfNSW

Quality reporting: Full transparency and accountability

CBS provides comprehensive, transparent quality reporting that gives TfNSW complete visibility of our performance and continuous improvement journey.

Your benefits from transparent reporting

This multi-layered reporting framework ensures TfNSW:

Maintains real-time visibility of quality performance

Receives early warning of potential issues

Sees demonstrable improvement through data-driven insights

Has complete confidence in our quality management capability

Every report demonstrates our commitment to transparency, accountability and continuous enhancement – turning quality metrics into actionable intelligence that drives superior outcomes for TfNSW.

Proven quality track record

CBS's quality excellence is demonstrated through measurable results:

TfNSW performance

4.5+/5.0 client satisfaction ratings consistently achieved across all projects

Zero major quality issues or deliverable rejections

$1.049B1 validated savings demonstrating analytical excellence

Positive stakeholder feedback on professionalism and responsiveness

Industry recognition

Award-winning CAPITAL Framework recognised by AMC and ISC

Long-term client retention built on quality and trust

Consistent excellence across technical and commercial domains

Cross-sector validation through $20B project portfolio

This proven track record confirms CBS's quality management system delivers measurable results, providing TfNSW with absolute confidence in our capability.

Identification and management of key risks associated with the services

Response synopsis:

CBS Group has identified five key risks associated with the services and developed comprehensive mitigation strategies for each: 

Risk 1 – Transition and mobilisation risk (mitigated through our incumbent position enabling 3-day mobilisation vs. competitors' 7–12 month learning curve)

Risk 2 – Knowledge loss and team continuity risk (mitigated through 100% retention of the same seven-person team over 18 months, eliminating personnel turnover risk) 

Risk 3 – Quality and deliverable risk (mitigated through our proven ISO 55000-aligned quality framework that has achieved zero major quality issues and 97.2% on-time delivery)

Risk 4 – Stakeholder alignment and governance risk (mitigated through our deep understanding of TfNSW's governance frameworks and established relationships across all relevant divisions)

Risk 5 – Value delivery and commercial risk (mitigated through the CAPITAL Framework's systematic approach that has delivered $1.049B1 in validated savings)  

Our risk management approach is proactive rather than reactive, identifying risks before they materialise and implementing controls embedded in our delivery methodology. All project risks are linked directly to TfNSW's strategic objectives and tracked through a comprehensive risk register that is reported quarterly. 

Risk management: proven expertise from current engagements

As TfNSW's established partner, CBS Group brings deep, practical understanding of Asset Management Support Services risks, informed by our active delivery on current asset management contracts where we successfully navigate these challenges daily.

Collaborative risk optimisation

We approach risk management as a collaborative partnership that balances cost, performance, and safety while aligning with your Strategic Asset Management Plan (SAMP) and ISO 55001 principles. Rather than simply managing risks, we transform them into opportunities for value creation.

Proactive, embedded approach

Our risk management philosophy emphasises:

Proactive identification before risks materialise

Shared accountability between CBS and TfNSW teams

Continuous assurance through systematic monitoring

The CAPITAL Framework embeds risk mitigation directly into every deliverable, from risk transfer models to Contract Management Plans. Through systems-based analysis and our Catalyst 7™ methodology for multi-dimensional value optimisation, we ensure risks are not just controlled but actively leveraged to support TfNSW's strategic objectives.

This proven approach has delivered exceptional risk-adjusted outcomes across our current engagements, providing TfNSW with confidence that risks will be expertly managed from Day 1.

Our risk management framework

CBS applies a systematic risk managemen

## CBS RFP WIP_RS 9 Pricing.docx

*File: `Working finals/RS9 Price/CBS RFP WIP_RS 9 Pricing.docx`*

Table of Contents

9.	Price	1

9.1.	Transition-In services	1

9.2.	Transition-out services	1

9.4.	Upper Limiting Fee	2

9.5.	Discounts and rebates	2

9.6.	Pricing Assumptions	2

Price 

CBS Group acknowledges all “Notes to the Respondent”.

Transition-In services

CBS Group anticipates transition in to be completed within 3 business days and will be lead by Kate Heath.   These services are provided at no charge to TfNSW.

Transition-out services

TfNSW may select one (or both) of the following two transition out options:

Option 1: A Traditional transition-out where nominated successors are identified and a transition of knowledge and support is scheduled over 4 weeks.   Costs associated with this option are limited to the CBS Transition Manager who is charged with:

confirming the scope of knowledge and skills to be transitioned

working with TfNSW to confirm the transition recipients for any ongoing works and associated document storage locations

development of a transition guide capturing the location of artefacts and status of works in progress

Option 2: As detailed Alternate Offer 3, creation of an instance of CBS Continuity that provides an enduring chatbot so that future TfNSW can continue to engage with the CBS team knowledge as established at the point of transition out.

Pricing for both options is provided in the following table, noting that in the event that more than one CBS staff member is retained, Transition Out is provided at a 50% cost reduction to the rates shown below.

Upper Limiting Fee

CBS Group's upper limiting fee is detailed below: 

Discounts and rebates

CBS Group will apply a discount of 2.5% to the escalated rates applied to any new purchase orders of greater than $1m issued. Purchase orders below this threshold do not attract this discount, and the initial fixed fee engagement does not attract this discount. 

Discounts will also apply under the same conditions in the event that Alternate Offer 1 is selected.

Pricing Assumptions

All travel for Melbourne based resources is incorporated into the rates and assuming prior travel requirements and frequencies are sustained.   Any material change in travel requirements will result in an adjustment to the rate or a request for travel costs.

## Alt 1_CBS VBP - FINAL DRAFT.docx

*File: `Working finals/Alternatives/Alt 1_CBS VBP - FINAL DRAFT.docx`*

1 December 2025

David Hanna
Strategic Sourcing Manager
Transport for NSW
Level 2, 7 Harvest Street
Macquarie Park NSW 2113

Subject: Alternate Offer 1 for Asset Management Services

Dear David,

On behalf of CBS Group, I am pleased to submit our alternate offer for the provision of Asset Management services to Transport for NSW. This proposal responds to our discussions regarding innovative commercial models and may be accepted either as an alternate to our fully compliant offer or as a supplemental engagement mechanism to meet TfNSW's asset management and value creation needs over the term.

The offer comprises two key elements:

Value Based Pricing

This framework offers a shared-risk model that directly aligns our compensation with the tangible value we deliver. It moves beyond the conventional client-consultant relationship—where value risk sits entirely with the client—to a collaborative partnership focused on measurable outcomes.

Collaborative Contract Terms

Our fully compliant offer provides for 100% acceptance of the proposed contract terms. However, these terms pass risks to CBS Group that come at a cost. We have assessed the value of terms representing additional risk and propose reduced fees where the amendments in this alternate offer are accepted.

We are confident this framework offers a compelling value proposition: it combines CBS Group's deep local expertise with global validation capabilities, enabling us to accept risk on value-based pricing while providing additional service value in exchange for collaborative commercial risk allocation.

We have enclosed a detailed proposal outlining the framework, its operation, and the value it is designed to create.

Thank you for the opportunity to present this alternate offer. We look forward to discussing it with you further.

Sincerely

Sarah Taylor
Partner, Strategy and Tech
CBS Group Pty Ltd

Executive summary

Situation: Traditional models of consultant engagement leave all value risk with the client. Consultants are paid for their time and deliverables regardless of whether their recommendations generate tangible benefits. This creates a fundamental misalignment of incentives—the consultant's commercial success is disconnected from the client's actual outcomes. Similarly, standard contract terms allocate risks to the consultant that come at a cost yet provide no corresponding benefit to the client when those risks do not materialise.

Complication: Value in complex infrastructure engagements is inherently uncertain. Even well-conceived initiatives may not deliver anticipated benefits due to implementation challenges, changing circumstances or unforeseen constraints. Under traditional arrangements, TfNSW bears this uncertainty entirely while consultants face no consequence for recommendations that fail to materialise as value. Meanwhile, contract terms that transfer risk to consultants simply result in higher fees to cover contingencies that may never eventuate—cost without benefit.

Response: CBS Group proposes an alternate engagement model built on two complementary mechanisms.

Traditional Engagement on Collaborative Contract Terms: This mechanism provides for engagement of our core and supplementary teams on amended commercial terms. TfNSW acceptance of these terms allows a reduction in CBS Group costs and risk premium - enabling reduced fees. This creates a direct exchange: TfNSW accepts manageable risks in return for tangible cost savings

Value Based Price (VBP) Method: Value Based Pricing structures our compensation around shared risk and aligned returns, operating on three foundational principles: shared conviction (both parties agree there is genuine possibility of value before work commences), co-investment (CBS Group assigns appropriate specialists on a fixed retainer basis, with value share agreed initiative-by-initiative), and aligned returns (if value is delivered, we share in it; if not, our returns are limited accordingly). 

We bring a suite of proven asset management products, methodologies and analytical tools that have delivered more than $1 billion in validated savings for TfNSW over the past three years. It is with this capability and track record that we ca accept shared risk with confidence.

Mechanism 1 is detailed at section 4 of this Alternate Proposal.  The remainder of this document has been drafted to explain the value and operation of Mechanism 2 (VBP).

We look forward to working through the our seed list of initiatives and the necessary commercial amendments to rapidly enable this model and start driving real value to support the Asset Financial Sustainability (AFS) targets set for the organisation.

Dual engagement pathway framework

Framework structure

This alternative framework is built on two distinct and complementary engagement pathways, allowing TfNSW to select the most suitable approach for any given initiative.

Method 1: Traditional, Time-Based Engagement

This pathway follows a conventional consultancy model. Services are provided at market rates for a defined scope of work. This option is suitable for projects where the scope and deliverables are clearly defined from the outset and where a traditional risk allocation is preferred. Services are provided at market rates for a defined scope of work. Work orders do not include value share provisions.

Method 2: Shared-Risk, Value-Based Engagement

This pathway is founded on a partnership model. CBSE accepts a significantly reduced monthly retainer, representing a substantial investment from our side. In return, we participate in the value we help create through a pre-agreed sharing mechanism on validated benefits. This model ensures that both organisations share in the risks and rewards, creating a strong alignment of interests.

Selection Criteria

The choice between Option A and Option B would be guided by the nature of the project.

Method 1 is the preferred choice for more straightforward engagements. This includes defined-scope studies, technical investigations, or when seeking short-term expert advice. It is also appropriate for engagements where a more conservative, traditional risk allocation is desired

Method 2 is particularly well-suited for initiatives with the potential for significant value creation, such as long-term transformation programs or projects that require innovative solutions. It is most effective in situations where a deep, collaborative partnership and fully aligned incentives are critical to success.

Flexibility and Combination

The framework is designed for flexibility, allowing TfNSW to:

Employ different options for different projects concurrently

Transition between the two options with an agreed-upon notice period

Combine both options within a single large-scale program

Initiate with a pilot of Option A before committing to a broader rollout

The Case for a New Engagement Model (Method 2)

The Problem with Traditional Consulting

The conventional consulting model contains a structural flaw that limits its effectiveness for infrastructure agencies. Under traditional fee-for-service arrangements, consultants are compensated for effort expended rather than value delivered.

This misalignment creates predictable consequences:

Infrastructure asset management initiatives involve inherent uncertainty. Even expertly designed interventions may not achieve intended outcomes due to organisational readiness, competing priorities, market conditions, or technical constraints that emerge during implementation. This uncertainty is not a failure of planning—it is a reality of complex systems.

The question is not whether to accept this uncertainty, but how to allocate it fairly between client and consultant. Under traditional models, TfNSW accepts all of this uncertainty while consultants face no consequence for recommendations that fail to materialise as value.

A Different Approach: Shared Risk, Aligned Returns

CBS Group proposes an alternative model structured around three foundational principles:

Shared conviction: Both parties agree there is genuine possibility of value in each initiative before work commences.

Co-investment: CBS Group assigns specialists on a retainer basis at 40-50% of market rates, with value, duration and value share agreed initiative-by-initiative.

Aligned returns: If value is delivered, we share in that value. If it is not, our returns are limited accordingly.

The following comparison illustrates how fundamentally this differs from traditional arrangements:

Risk and Reward Distribution

The model's value becomes clearer when examining how outcomes translate to returns:

Why CBS Group Can Accept This Risk

Traditional consultants cannot easily adopt value-based models because they lack the tools to assess value potential with confidence. CBS Group's position is different.

Our willingness to share value risk is underpinned by a proven toolkit developed through successful delivery across major infrastructure projects including the Western Harbour Tunnel, Sydney Harbour Tunnel, and M6 Stage 1:

These capabilities do not eliminate uncertainty—they reduce it to a level where shared risk becomes commercially viable.

Partnership Foundation

This proposal builds upon CBS Group's established relationship with TfNSW, spanning over 20 years of engagement with NSW transport infrastructure. Our current integration of CBS specialists within TfNSW has demonstrated our critical thinking, pragmatic technical and commercial solutions, and ability to engage with a wide variety of stakeholders.

To enhance the credibility of our value-based model, we have established a strategic alliance with ST Engineering. Their role is to provide:

Independent validation of benefits claimed

Global benchmarking against international standards

Technology assessments based on worldwide deployments

Importantly, CBS Group remains the sole contracting party—we manage all coordination with ST Engineering, maintaining a single interface for TfNSW throughout the engagement.

Market Validation with ST Engineering

ST Engineering provides validation services that enhance the credibility of our value-based model without adding complexity to the TfNSW relationship. 

ST Engineering will provide a market lense on initiatives, enabling CBS Group analysis to be validated, supported and benchmarked by a global industry leader with Australian and international experience in delivery and operations of transport infrastructure.

The following principles govern this partnership:

CBS Group remains the sole contracting party with full accountability for all deliverables

ST Engineering provides advisory and validation services only, with no direct TfNSW interface

All ST Engineering services are included within CBS Group pricing

CBS Group interprets and applies all recommendations, maintaining delivery simplicity

The Value-Based Engagement Model

Core Principles

The value-based engagement model fundamentally restructures the consultant-client relationship around three principles that address the limitations of traditional arrangements:

Principle 1: Shared Conviction

Before any initiative commences, both TfNSW and CBS Group must agree that a genuine possibility of value exists. This is not a formality—it requires both parties to review the proposed intervention, examine the evidence base, and commit resources only where there is mutual confidence in the potential for measurable improvement.

Principle 2: Co-Investment

CBS Group assigns a team of appropriate specialists to agreed initiatives on a retainer basis. The value target, task duration and value share percentage are agreed on an initiative-by-initiative basis. CBS Group's initial fees are set at 40-50% of market rates, representing our investment in the partnership. This reduced fee is not a discount—it reflects our willingness to accept a lower certain return in exchange for participation in value upside.

Principle 3: Aligned Returns

If value is delivered and independently validated, CBS Group shares in that value through benefit-sharing payments. If value does not materialise, our returns are limited to the reduced retainer. This structure ensures that CBS Group's commercial success depends directly on TfNSW's outcomes.

Process Overview 

The value-based pricing model operates as illustrated in the diagram and incorporating the following steps:

Client or CBS identifies a value-generating opportunity

CBS and TfNSW agree on scope, baseline costs, method of measurement (considering CATALYST 7™), start and end date, number of teams, and value share (with cap if required)

A Project Schedule is completed and executed by both parties

Project commences with monthly retainers for the agreed number of teams paid by TfNSW

Value share is paid once measurement outcomes are confirmed as documented in the Project Schedule

Identification & Scoping

Either party may identify a potential value-generating opportunity. CBS Group will maintain an ongoing pipeline of opportunities through its embedded team and strategic analysis.

For each identified opportunity, CBS Group and TfNSW will jointly:

Define the problem statement and anticipated outcome

Establish baseline costs or performance metrics

Agree the measurement methodology aligned with CATALYST 7™

Determine the number of teams and resource commitment required

Agree the start date, end date, and value measurement period

Negotiate the value share percentage and any applicable cap

Agree Terms and Execute Schedule

The agreed terms will be documented in a Project Schedule, which forms a binding appendix to the Master Agreement. Work on the initiative will not commence until both parties have executed the Project Schedule.

Delivery

Upon execution of the Project Schedule:

Monthly retainers for the agreed number of teams commence

Baseline data is formally documented and certified

The project team is mobilised in accordance with the agreed composition

Realise Value 

Upon completion of the initiative:

Value is measured in accordance with the agreed methodology

Benefits are validated through the applicable validation tier

Value share is calculated and paid as documented in the Project Schedule

Project Schedule Requirements

Before work commences on any value-based initiative, both parties will agree and document the following in a Project Schedule (Appendix A):

Scope: The specific activities, deliverables and outcomes for the initiative

Baseline: The current state costs or performance metrics against which value will be measured

Measurement Method: The methodology for calculating value, aligned with the CATALYST 7™ framework

Duration: The start date, end date, and value measurement period

Team Composition: The number and composition of teams assigned to the initiative

Value Share: The percentage payable to CBS Group, including any applicable cap

Retainer Framework

The value-based model operates through initiative-specific retainers, with the number of teams determined for each Project Schedule based on the scope and complexity of the work.

Base Retainer Terms

Team Allocation

The number of teams assigned to each initiative will be agreed in the Project Schedule based on:

Scope and complexity of the initiative

Expected duration

Target value and delivery timeframes

Resource requirements identified during scoping

Multiple initiatives may run concurrently, each with its own team allocation and Project Schedule.

Minimum Deliverables Within Retainer

Each monthly retainer includes:

Monthly performance dashboard and benefit tracking report

Access to CATALYST 7™ tools and templates for engaged projects

Monthly stakeholder briefing and progress review

Continuous monitoring of asset performance against established baselines

Value Share Mechanism

Value share is calculated and paid on an initiative-by-initiative basis. For each value-generating opportunity, CBS Group and TfNSW will execute a Project Schedule that documents the specific commercial terms applicable to that initiative.

The value share payable for each initiative is calculated as follows:

Value Share = (Validated Benefits - Cumulative Retainer Paid) × Agreed Value Share Percentage

The value share is payable only after the measurement outcomes are confirmed through the validation process documented in the Project Schedule.

Commercial Framework

The commercial structure operationalises these principles through an integrated pricing arrangement:

This structure creates a partnership dynamic fundamentally different from traditional consulting. CBS Group has a direct financial interest in ensuring that initiatives succeed—not merely that deliverables are completed.

Validation Framework

Validation Principles

The validation process is governed by the following principles to ensure both rigour and efficiency:

Transparency: All benefit calculations and supporting evidence are shared with TfNSW

Conservatism: Where uncertainty exists, benefit estimates err on the conservative side

Attribution: Only benefits demonstrably linked to CBS Group interventions are included

Timing: Benefits are recognised when realised, not when projected

Independence: ST Engineering provides objective assessment at all tiers

Tiered Validation Approach

A robust validation framework is essential to the integrity of the value-based model. Both parties must have confidence that claimed benefits are real, measurable and attributable to the engagement. We propose a tiered validation approach that applies appropriate scrutiny proportionate to benefit magnitude.

The following table sets out the validation requirements at each tier (may be amended in the project schedule by agreement):

Benchmarking and Global Comparison

ST Engineering's involvement enables benefits to be contextualised against international standards. This benchmarking serves two purposes: it validates that claimed improvements are genuine by reference to what has been achieved elsewhere, and it identifies further opportunities by highlighting gaps between current performance and global best practice.

Initial (Seed) Initiatives for Consideration

Seed List of Value-Based Initiatives

CBS Group has developed an initial seed list of initiatives for consideration by TfNSW under the value-based engagement model. Each initiative has been assessed for value potential, implementation complexity and alignment with TfNSW strategic priorities. The value, duration and value share for each initiative would be agreed prior to commencement in accordance with the model principles.

The following table presents the proposed initiatives:

Initiative Selection and Prioritisation

The initiatives listed above represent opportunities identified through CBS Group's existing engagement with TfNSW and analysis of strategic priorities. Not all initiatives need proceed simultaneously—the value-based model allows for selective engagement based on TfNSW priorities and resource availability.

We propose the following approach to initiative selection:

Joint prioritisation workshop: CBS Group and TfNSW jointly review the seed list and agree priority initiatives

Value assessment: For each priority initiative, both parties review the value hypothesis and agree target outcomes

Commercial agreement: Value target, duration, and value share percentage are agreed for each initiative

Phased commencement: Initiatives commence in agreed sequence, allowing learnings to inform subsequent engagements

Traditional Engagement Under Collaborative Terms

To enable engagement of our core and supplementary teams at a reduced set of rates, the proposed amendments to the contract detailed at Appendix C Table 1 must be accepted.

To enable the value-based engagement model amendments to the contract detailed at Appendix C Table 2 must be accepted.

Upon acceptance of the terms at Appendix C, the schedule or rates at Appendix D can be applied.

These amendments are designed to be applied to TfNSW's Professional Services Contract (Version 4.0, September 2024) as provided in Annexure A - Proposed Contract.

Next Steps

Engagement Process

We propose the following progression to establish the value-based partnership:

Our Commitment

CBS Group commits to fostering a genuine partnership with shared success, delivering validated and measurable value, maintaining single-point accountability throughout the engagement, providing global insights with local delivery, and creating alignment of interests that benefits both parties.

This partnership proposal is valid for 90 days and represents CBS Group's commitment to deliver world-class, validated asset management solutions through a commercial model that ensures our success depends on yours.

Appendix E: Catalyst7TM Measurement Framework

CBS Group Principles for Using CATALYST 7™ and Applying Value-Based Pricing

Overview

CATALYST 7™ is CBS Group's proprietary framework for delivering and measuring value across infrastructure advisory engagements. Developed through over a decade of application and more than $1 billion in documented savings for Transport for NSW and other major infrastructure clients, CATALYST 7™ provides a comprehensive methodology that moves beyond traditional time-and-materials consulting to create strategic partnerships where compensation is directly tied to measurable value creation.

The framework recognises that infrastructure value extends far beyond financial returns to encompass seven interconnected capital dimensions, enabling CBS Group to identify, quantify, and deliver holistic value that benefits all stakeholders.

The Seven Capitals Framework

CATALYST 7™ measures and optimises value across seven interconnected capital dimensions:

Value Multiplication Principles

CATALYST 7™ recognises that the seven capitals form an interconnected system where interventions in one dimension create ripple effects across others. CBS Group actively designs interventions to maximise synergistic effects through three mechanisms:

Cross-Capital Synergies: Interventions in one capital dimension enhance value creation in another (e.g., Digital Capital investments enhance Manufactured Capital performance while improving Natural Capital outcomes).

Cascading Value Effects: Value created in one dimension triggers sequential value creation in others through mapped pathways.

Reinforcing Feedback Loops: Value creation across multiple dimensions establishes self-reinforcing cycles that compound over time.

Typical value multipliers range from 1.8x–2.7x for two-dimensional interactions, 2.5x–3.8x for three-dimensional synergies, and 3.2x–4.7x for comprehensive multi-dimensional integration.

Value-Based Pricing Commercial Model

Dual-Component Structure

CBS Group's VBP model comprises two components designed to balance budget predictability with strong performance incentives:

Strategic Partnership Retainer: Fixed monthly fee covering strategic advisory, methodology development, knowledge transfer, governance support, and continuous improvement activities.

Value-Based Success Fees: Calculated as a percentage of independently validated value creation across the seven capitals, typically representing 60–80% of total CBS compensation in successful engagements.

Measurement and Validation

CATALYST 7™ applies rigorous measurement protocols to ensure credible value creation claims:

Baseline Rigor: Comprehensive baseline assessment incorporating 3–5 years of historical performance data, industry benchmarks, and explicit counterfactual analysis.

Conservative Estimation: Lower-bound estimates, time-value discounting, and exclusion of weakly attributed value categories.

Independent Validation: All material value claims (>$1M) undergo third-party validation by qualified assessors, with full methodology and data transparency.

Performance Guarantees

CBS Group typically guarantees minimum value creation of 3x–5x total retainer fees over the engagement period, demonstrating confidence in the CATALYST 7™ methodology. Remediation mechanisms include success fee waivers, partial retainer refunds, and extended engagement commitments if guaranteed minimums are not achieved.

Perfect Incentive Alignment

CATALYST 7™ VBP fundamentally restructures the consultant-client relationship:

Outcome-Based Compensation: CBS Group's compensation is calculated as a percentage of documented value creation, ensuring CBS earns more when clients achieve more value.

Risk-Sharing: CBS Group bears significant performance risk—if interventions fail to create value, CBS receives only the base retainer without success fees.

Long-Term Partnership: Engagements typically span 3–5 years with phased success fee payments tied to sustained value creation.

Capability Transfer: Structured knowledge transfer builds client capabilities over time, enabling increasingly independent value creation.

Summary

CATALYST 7™ represents a fundamental shift from traditional consulting to genuine strategic partnership. By measuring and optimising value across seven interconnected capitals, applying rigorous baseline and validation protocols, and structuring commercial arrangements around shared risk and reward, CBS Group delivers transformative outcomes where consultant success depends entirely on delivering measurable, sustainable client value.

Appendix F: VBP Standards Rationalisation Approach

Executive Summary

This appendix describes a value-based initiative for systematic review and rationalisation of TfNSW Standards, operating within the VBP framework established in the main proposal. The initiative addresses three objectives: reducing the compliance burden on projects, reducing standards maintenance costs for TfNSW, and applying a risk-based methodology to enable innovation.

The approach involves identifying a target asset class, mapping TfNSW standards against existing Australian and international standards to reduce TfNSW-unique content to genuinely unique requirements, then identifying opportunities to explicitly allow creative design solutions that implement whole-of-life innovations.

The intended outcomes are less unique standards volume to manage (with associated internal cost reduction), reduced compliance and project costs, and greater opportunity for innovation with reduced lock-in to past precedents that may no longer serve TfNSW's interests.

1. Relationship to the VBP Framework

1.1 Integration with Method 2 Engagement

The Standards Innovation Programme operates as a specific initiative under the Method 2 (Value-Based) engagement pathway described in Section 4 of this alternate proposal. As with all VBP initiatives, this programme follows the established process comprising identification and scoping, agreement of terms and execution of a Project Schedule, delivery, and value realisation.

The three foundational principles that govern all Method 2 engagements apply equally to this initiative:

Shared Conviction: Both TfNSW and CBS Group must agree that genuine value creation potential exists in standards rationalisation before work commences.

Co-Investment: CBS Group undertakes analysis and development work at reduced retainer rates, reflecting our investment in the partnership.

Aligned Returns: If value is delivered and independently validated, CBS Group shares in that value. If value does not materialise, our returns are limited to the reduced retainer.

1.2 Application of CATALYST 7™

Standards rationalisation creates value across multiple capital dimensions within the CATALYST 7™ framework:

2. Objectives

The Standards Rationalisation initiative addresses three distinct but related objectives:

Reduce Compliance Burden: Simplify standards to reduce the compliance effort required on projects, lowering project delivery costs and timelines.

Reduce Standards Maintenance Cost: Decrease the volume of TfNSW-unique standards requiring ongoing maintenance, freeing internal resources currently dedicated to standards administration.

Enable Innovation: Apply a risk-based methodology to remove unnecessary prescription and explicitly allow creative design solutions that deliver whole-of-life value.

2.1 The Problem

TfNSW maintains a substantial portfolio of transport standards, many of which duplicate or exceed requirements already established in Australian or international standards. This creates several challenges:

Unnecessary Uniqueness: Standards that replicate AS/ISO requirements with minor TfNSW variations create compliance complexity without commensurate benefit.

Maintenance Burden: Each unique standard requires ongoing review, update, and stakeholder management—consuming internal resources.

Innovation Barriers: Prescriptive specifications prevent adoption of proven technologies and lock projects into past precedents that may no longer represent best practice.

Project Cost Impact: Compliance with duplicative or overly prescriptive standards adds cost to projects without delivering proportionate value.

3. Approach

The initiative follows a two-stage approach, commencing with a target asset class to focus the rationalisation effort.

3.1 Target Asset Class Selection

The initiative commences with selection of a target asset class to focus the rationalisation effort. Selection criteria include volume of applicable standards, potential for cost reduction, alignment with TfNSW strategic priorities, and readiness for implementation. The recommended starting point would be agreed with TfNSW during Project Schedule development.

3.2 Stage 1: Standards Mapping and Reduction

The first stage maps TfNSW standards against existing Australian (AS) and international (ISO, EN) standards to identify where TfNSW requirements duplicate or marginally vary from established frameworks. The objective is to reduce TfNSW-unique content to genuinely unique requirements—those elements where TfNSW's operating context necessitates departure from industry norms.

This stage involves the following activities:

Map each TfNSW standard against corresponding AS/ISO/EN frameworks

Identify clauses that duplicate or marginally vary from referenced standards

Assess whether TfNSW-specific variations are justified by genuine operational requirements

Recommend consolidation to reference external standards where appropriate

Retain only genuinely unique TfNSW requirements within rationalised standards

3.3 Stage 2: Enabling Innovation

The second stage identifies opportunities within rationalised standards to explicitly allow creative design solutions that implement whole-of-life (WoL) innovations. This involves shifting from prescriptive specifications (which lock in past precedents) to performance-based requirements (which define outcomes and allow flexibility in how those outcomes are achieved).

This stage involves the following activities:

Identify prescriptive clauses that constrain innovation without commensurate risk mitigation

Apply risk-based assessment to determine where flexibility can be safely introduced

Draft performance-based alternatives that define required outcomes rather than mandated methods

Create pathways for adoption of proven technologies (e.g., sustainable materials, smart systems)

Validate proposed changes through stakeholder engagement and pilot implementation

3.4 Stage 3

Work through the formal standards change and concession processes ensuring technical operational and custodian input is sought and accommodated and that the value is realised.

Communicate the change to impacted projects and asset stewards to ensure that the value is realised in project delivery and operations phases.

4. Commercial Model

4.1 Alignment with VBP Framework

The commercial structure for this initiative follows the Method 2 framework established in the main proposal. CBS Group accepts below-market retainer rates in exchange for participation in validated value creation. This creates the same partnership dynamic that characterises all VBP engagements—CBS Group has a direct financial interest in ensuring that standards optimisations succeed, not merely that deliverables are completed.

4.2 Value Share Calculation

Consistent with Section 4.5 of the main proposal, the value share for this initiative is calculated as:

Value Share = (Validated Benefits − Cumulative Retainer Paid) × Agreed Value Share Percentage

For this initiative, the proposed value share is 20% of independently verified cost savings, with TfNSW retaining 80% of all validated benefits. This allocation reflects the significant risk CBS Group accepts by undertaking development work at reduced rates.

4.3 Validation Approach

Benefits validation follows the tiered approach established in Section 4.7 of the main proposal, with ST Engineering providing independent assessment at all tiers. Standards rationalisation benefits are measured through:

Independent quantity surveyor assessment comparing costs under revised standards versus baseline

Net benefit calculation after accounting for all implementation costs

ST Engineering global benchmarking to validate that claimed improvements align with international precedents

Audit rights for both parties ensuring transparency in all calculations

5. Implementation

5.1 Project Schedule Requirements

Consistent with Section 4.3 of the main proposal, implementation commences upon execution of a Project Schedule documenting:

Scope and deliverables (specific standards to be addressed in each phase)

Baseline costs or performance metrics (current cost profile under existing standards)

Value measurement methodology aligned with CATALYST 7™

Start date, end date, and measurement period

Team composition and resource commitment

Value share percentage (20%) and any applicable cap

Validation tier and process per main proposal clause 4.7

Governance and reporting requirements

5.2 Risk Allocation

The risk allocation for this initiative follows the shared-risk principles of Method 2:

Development Risk (CBS Group): CBS Group bears all costs of standards analysis, drafting, and stakeholder engagement. If standards are not adopted, CBS Group receives only the reduced retainer.

Implementation Risk (Shared): Both parties share accountability for successful implementation through joint governance mechanisms.

Value Realisation Risk (Shared): Value share is payable only upon independent validation of benefits, ensuring both parties are aligned on outcomes.

6. Intended Outcomes

Successful delivery of this initiative will achieve the following outcomes:

6.1 Reduced Standards Volume

By mapping TfNSW standards to AS/ISO frameworks and retaining only genuinely unique requirements, the initiative will significantly reduce the volume of TfNSW-unique standards. This delivers:

Lower Maintenance Costs: Fewer unique standards require ongoing review, update, and stakeholder management

Freed Internal Resources: Staff currently dedicated to standards administration can be redeployed to higher-value activities

Simplified Governance: Reference to external standards transfers maintenance responsibility and ensures automatic alignment with industry developments

6.2 Reduced Compliance Costs

Rationalised standards with clearer requirements and reduced TfNSW-unique content will lower compliance burden on projects:

Lower Project Costs: Reduced effort required to demonstrate compliance with simplified standards

Faster Delivery: Less time spent navigating complex or duplicative requirements

Clearer Expectations: Industry participants can more readily understand and meet TfNSW requirements

6.3 Greater Innovation Opportunity

Performance-based specifications that explicitly allow creative design solutions will unlock innovation:

Reduced Lock-in: Removal of prescriptive requirements based on past precedents that may no longer represent best practice

Whole-of-Life Value: Flexibility to adopt solutions optimised for lifecycle cost rather than lowest capital cost

Technology Adoption: Pathways for proven technologies (sustainable materials, smart systems, digital infrastructure) that are currently constrained

Competitive Tension: Market can propose innovative solutions, driving better value outcomes for TfNSW

7. Next Steps

Subject to TfNSW's interest in proceeding with this initiative, the following steps would apply:

Joint identification of target asset class for initial focus

Development and execution of Project Schedule per main proposal Section 4

Commencement of Stage 1 (Standards Mapping and Reduction)

Progression to Stage 2 (Enabling Innovation) upon completion of mapping

This initiative represents a practical application of the VBP framework to deliver structural improvements in TfNSW's standards environment. The shared-risk model ensures CBS Group's commercial success depends on achieving the intended outcomes: less standards volume, lower compliance costs, and greater innovation opportunity.


| --- | --- | --- |

| Characteristic | Traditional Model | Consequence for TfNSW |

| Payment basis | Time and materials or fixed fee for deliverables | Payment made regardless of whether recommendations generate value |

| Risk allocation | Client bears all implementation and realisation risk | TfNSW absorbs losses if initiatives fail to deliver anticipated benefits |

| Consultant incentive | Maximise billable hours or scope | Focus shifts to engagement extension rather than rapid value delivery |

| Success measurement | Deliverable completion | Reports and recommendations may sit unimplemented |

| Knowledge transfer | Limited incentive to build client capability | Dependency on consultant for ongoing support |


| --- | --- | --- |

| Dimension | Traditional Model | Value-Based Model |

| Payment trigger | Time expended or deliverables completed | Value delivered and validated |

| Risk allocation | Client bears all value risk | Risk shared between parties |

| Consultant investment | None—full fees paid regardless of outcome | 50-60% of market rate deferred, contingent on value |

| Success definition | Scope completion | Measurable improvement in outcomes |

| Alignment of interests | Limited—consultant paid whether client benefits or not | Strong—consultant succeeds only when client succeeds |

| Validation requirement | None | Independent verification required |

| Knowledge transfer | Optional, often limited | Essential—builds client capability to sustain benefits |


| --- | --- | --- |

| Scenario | Traditional Model | Value-Based Model |

| Initiative delivers $2M in benefits | Consultant receives full fee; TfNSW retains all benefit | Consultant receives reduced retainer plus 30% of validated benefits; both parties benefit |

| Initiative delivers no measurable benefit | Consultant receives full fee; TfNSW absorbs loss | Consultant receives only reduced retainer; both parties share disappointment |

| Initiative partially succeeds ($500K benefits) | Consultant receives full fee; TfNSW receives modest return | Consultant receives reduced retainer plus proportionate share; returns match outcomes |


| --- | --- | --- |

| Capability | Description | Value Contribution |

| CAPITAL Framework | A sophisticated asset management and commercial framework that drives asset performance, industry capability development, and whole-of-life cost certainty | Validated methodology with $180M+ savings on Western Harbour Tunnel alone |

| Lifecycle Cost Modelling | Comprehensive analysis influencing design and operational decisions | Enables accurate benefit quantification at initiative outset |

| Operations Asset Management and Maintenance Strategies | Facilitation and formalisation of operation and maintenance concepts to ensure assets meet required needs with appropriate frameworks in place | Reduced waste in build, greater performance in operate phase |

| Co-Lab Framework | A methodology that validates community service and safety needs to challenge lazy precedents and ensures capex and opex savings through rationalised asset design | Asset rationalisation, reduced whole-of-life cost, improved performance |

| Performance Benchmarking | Comparison against international best practices | Identifies realistic improvement targets with evidence base |

| Collaborative Teams | Application of ISO44001 to establish productive and enduring teams delivering on long-term contracts | Reduced contract management friction, greater contract performance |

| Asset Management Systems | A library of templates readily applied to long-term contracts, enabling TfNSW to transition to assurance-based contract management sooner | Improved contractor performance and reduced contract management overhead |


| --- | --- |

| Element | Standard Terms |

| Retainer per team per month | $10,000 (excl. GST) |

| Maximum specialists per team | 3 (typically: senior lead, technical specialist, analyst) |

| Payment timing | In advance, by the 1st business day of each month |

| Payment status | Non-refundable, representing mutual commitment |


| --- | --- |

| Element | Value-Based Model |

| Initial investment | 40-50% of market rates |

| Monthly retainer | $10,000 (non-refundable) |

| Validation services | Included (via ST Engineering if applicable) |

| Benefit share | Agreed share of validated benefits above retainer |

| Payment frequency | Quarterly reconciliation based on validated achievements |


| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Value Range | Validation Method | Review Period | ST Engineering Role | Cost Sharing |

| < $100,000 | Self-certification with pre agreed documentation evidence | 3 business days | Review and endorse | CBS Group funds |

| $100,000 – $250,000 | Self-certification with pre agreed documentation evidence and peer review | 5 business days | Review and endorse | CBS Group funds |

| $250,000 – $500,000 | External review accepted by nominated TfNSW Representative | 10 business days | Validate and benchmark | CBS Group funds |

| > $500,000 | As agreed in the Project Schedule | 20 business days | As agreed in the Project Schedule | As agree in the Project Schedule |


| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| No. | Initiative | Description | Expected Duration | Value Range |

| 1 | Standards Rationalisation | Systematic review and rationalisation of  TfNSW Standards to
reduce the compliance burden
reduce the standards maintenance cost 
Apply a risk based methodology to enable innovation 
 Refer to Appendix F for comprehensive details | 4 weeks per asset class | $1m |

| 2 | Gate 1/2 Review | From the more than $1b in projects in gate 1/2, target those with greatest opportunity for cost reduction.
Apply CBS Co-Lab methodology and leadership with Egis industry context and expertise to:
Validate project needs and remove un-necessary elements
Validate the specifications to identify opportunities to depart from precedents that are driving un-necessary WoL Cost
Support project concessions on a Value for Money basis | 12 weeks for a package of 3-5 projects | Target 10-15% of project value |

| 3 | Projects in Procurement | For projects in procurement, assign a team to support the process of gaining market supported standards concessions to allow industry innovations to be accepted.
The CBSE team will
Review innovations proposed during project procurement
Assess those using the Co-Lab framework to identify those that could be accepted with a justifiable standards concession or amendment to the project brief. | 4-8  weeks per project | Target 10-15% of project value |

| 4 | AM System Template + Guide | Creation of standardised asset management system templates and implementation guides for consistent application across TfNSW asset classes and delivery partners.
Reduce the cost of contract assurance for TfNSW | 3-4 months | $3m |

| 5 | SHT OMCS Delivery | CBS will provide a project team and agree a project budget, schedule and success criteria and deliver the project under budget and schedule | 18 months | $3m below current budget |

| 6 | Needs-Risk Model | Development of an integrated needs assessment and risk-based prioritisation model for asset renewal and capital investment, enabling evidence-based allocation of limited funding.
This initiative will provide the necessary framework for asset rationalisation in many contexts | 6-9 months | $800K - $2M |

| 7 | Coffs Harbour ByPass M&E Rationalisation | Applying the CoLab framework and risk based analysis work with the project team to descope works on M&E thatmay not be required – noting the practical and commercial limits that may apply to the project in delivery | 6-9 months | $800K - $2M |

| 8 | CRN Property Portfolio | CBS takes risk on the development and procurement of a solution for the CRN property portfolio that has a WoL cost to TfNSW below current costs.
CBS will lead the development of the packaging strategy, the market engagement, and the procurement activities.
TfNSW will retain approval and transition responsibility | 6-9 months | $5m |

| 9 | PA/RRB Replacement | Retirement of RRB and PA standards and the associated transition to modern evacuation strategies and systems for associated reduction in maintenance and program savings for M6, SHT and WHT. | 4-6 months | $500K - $1.5M |

| 10 | Energy Savings Certificate (ESC) Revenue | Using CBS Group and partners and Sydney Trains success, identify energy saving initiatives (planned and in progress) that may entitle TfNSW to generate revenue from ESCs.   CBS can lead this entire process from identification, measurement, certificate generation and trading for best return | 6-24 months | >$50m |


| --- | --- | --- |

| Phase | Timing | Activities |

| Framework Agreement | Weeks 1-2 | Executive briefing on value-based model; ST Engineering validation methodology review; selection of preferred commercial structure; initial product prioritisation |

| Commercial Finalisation | Weeks 3-4 | Contract amendments completion; governance structure establishment; validation criteria agreement; baseline methodology confirmation |

| Partnership Launch | Weeks 5-6 | Team mobilisation including ST Engineering briefing; stakeholder communication; initial benchmarking study; commencement of first initiative |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| No. | Topic | Proposed Amendment | Reason | Category |

| 1 | Intellectual Property – Pre-existing IP (Clause 4.1) | Amend clause 4.1(c) to read: "The Contractor grants to the Principal a non-exclusive, irrevocable, perpetual, transferable, royalty-free licence to use, reproduce, communicate, and modify or incorporate into other works (and sub-licence any third party to do so) the Contractor's Pre-existing IPR to the extent incorporated in the Deliverables as required by the Principal to receive, use and otherwise enjoy the full benefit of the Services." | The current drafting grants TfNSW an unrestricted licence to all CBS Group pre-existing IP used in connection with the engagement, rather than limiting the licence to IP actually incorporated in deliverables. This could inadvertently transfer rights to CBS Group's frameworks and methodology, proprietary asset management tools, and analytical models that are merely used in performing services but not delivered. The proposed amendment preserves TfNSW's full benefit of deliverables whilst protecting CBS Group's core IP. | IP Protection |

| 2 | Intellectual Property – New IP Ownership (Clause 4.2) | Add new sub-clause 4.2(c): "Notwithstanding clause 4.2(a), where the Contractor creates methodologies, frameworks, tools, templates or know-how of general application that are not specific to the Principal's requirements (General Methodologies), such General Methodologies shall remain the property of the Contractor, with the Principal granted a perpetual, non-exclusive, royalty-free licence to use such General Methodologies for NSW transport purposes." | The blanket assignment of all New IPR to TfNSW would capture improvements CBS Group makes to its frameworks and other proprietary methodologies during the engagement. Asset management consulting inherently involves continuous methodology refinement. The proposed amendment distinguishes between client-specific deliverables (which should vest in TfNSW) and general methodological improvements (which should remain with CBS Group). | IP Protection |

| 3 | Limitation of Liability – Exclusions (Clause 12.2) | Amend clause 12.2(a)(ii) to read: "liability under clause 4 (Intellectual Property Rights) for which the Contractor's aggregate liability shall be limited to 200% of fees paid under this Contract , 12.3 (Indemnities), 13 (Privacy and protection of Principal Data), 14 (Confidentiality) or 17 (Work health and safety and Workplace Laws);" | The current drafting carves out IP liability entirely from the liability cap. Given the broad IP assignment provisions and the nature of professional advisory services (where IP issues may arise from good faith disagreements over ownership), unlimited IP liability creates disproportionate exposure. The proposed sub-cap provides meaningful protection for TfNSW whilst capping CBS Group's maximum exposure. | Risk Allocation |

| 4 | Indemnities – Proportionality (Clause 12.3) | Amend opening words of clause 12.3 to read: "Subject to clause 12.4, the Contractor indemnifies..." And insert new clause 12.3(f): "The indemnities in clauses 12.3(a) to (e) are subject to the limitations in clause 12.1 except to the extent arising from the Contractor's fraud, wilful misconduct, or criminal conduct." | The current indemnity provisions are uncapped and fall outside the liability limitation. For professional services, uncapped indemnities are inconsistent with insurability and standard market practice. The proposed amendment subjects indemnities to the overall liability cap whilst carving out egregious conduct, providing proportionate protection for both parties. | Risk Allocation |

| 5 | Termination for Convenience – Payment (Clause 10.4) | Amend clause 10.4(b)(iii) to add sub-paragraph (C): "(C) a termination payment equal to [10]% of the fees that would have been payable for Services in the three months following termination, being compensation for the Contractor's costs of repositioning resources and lost opportunity." | The current termination for convenience provisions entitle CBS Group only to payment for work completed and authorised third-party costs. For an engagement requiring dedicated specialist resources, sudden termination causes material harm through stranded capacity and lost opportunity costs. The proposed amendment provides modest compensation reflecting market practice for specialist engagements whilst preserving TfNSW's termination right. | Commercial Terms |

| 6 | Insurance – Professional Indemnity Run-off (Clause 5.1) | Amend clause 5.1(b) to read: "professional indemnity insurance specified in the Key Details, such insurance to be maintained until the expiry of 3 years following completion of the relevant Contractor's Activities;" | The current requirement for 6 years' run-off cover exceeds market standard for professional services of this duration and scope. Given the advisory nature of the engagement and typical policy availability, 3 years provides adequate protection aligned with limitation periods for professional negligence whilst being commercially practical for a small consulting practice. | Insurance |


| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| No. | Clause Reference | Current Provision | Proposed Amendment | Rationale |

| 1 | 2.1 Term | Standard fixed term with option periods | Add: "For Option A engagements, the initial term shall be 2 years with performance gates at 6, 12 and 18 months. Subject to achieving agreed performance metrics, the term may be extended for 2 x 2-year periods. Performance gates will assess: value delivered, milestone achievement, and stakeholder satisfaction. Either party may exit at performance gates if minimum thresholds are not met." | Value-based engagements require time for benefits to materialise. Performance gates provide TfNSW with structured exit opportunities whilst enabling CBS Group to demonstrate value over an appropriate horizon. |

| 2 | 9.1 All work included | Fixed price includes all work | Replace with: "For Option A (Value-Based): Monthly retainer covers base services. Value share payments calculated separately based on validated benefits. For Option B (Traditional): Standard all-inclusive pricing applies." | The dual-pathway model requires distinct treatment of value-based retainer/share payments and traditional fixed pricing to operate effectively. |

| 3 | 9.2 Payment | Standard payment terms | Add: "Option A payments: (a) Monthly retainer paid in advance by 1st business day; (b) Value share paid within 30 days of benefit validation. Option B: Standard 30-day payment terms." | Clear payment timing is essential for cash flow certainty under the value-based model, recognising the different nature of retainer and performance-based payments. |

| 4 | 9.3 Invoices | Standard invoice requirements | Add: "Option A value share invoices must include: benefit calculation methodology, validation evidence, and independent verification (where required under clause 12.5)." | Transparency and auditability of value share calculations is essential for both parties' confidence in the model. |

| 5 | New Clause 12.5 Value Measurement | Not currently addressed | Add new clause: "12.5 Value Measurement: Parties agree to the CATALYST 7™ methodology for benefit calculation using pre-agreed templates. Streamlined validation thresholds apply: (a) <$100k: self-certification; (b) $100k-$250k: expedited peer review (5 business days); (c) $250k-$500k: standard peer review; (d) >$500k: independent validation. Templates for common benefit types will be agreed at commencement." | Efficient validation processes are essential for model operation. Tiered thresholds reduce administrative burden for smaller initiatives whilst maintaining rigour for larger value claims. |

| 6 | New Clause 12.6 Governance Structure | Not currently addressed | Add new clause: "12.6 Partnership Governance: A Joint Steering Committee shall be established with equal representation from both parties. The Committee shall conduct monthly operational reviews, quarterly value assessments, and annual partnership evaluations. Decisions regarding initiative prioritisation, value validation disputes, and framework modifications shall be made by the Committee." | Partnership governance provides TfNSW with visibility and shared control over the engagement, supporting collaborative value delivery and dispute prevention. |

| 7 | 12.1 Liability | Standard liability provisions | Add: "For Option A engagements: (a) Liability for retainer services is capped at the annual retainer paid under the relevant Project Schedule; (b) The Contractor bears no liability for value share amounts where validated benefits are not achieved, provided the Contractor has applied its methodology in accordance with Good Industry Practice; (c) The Contractor's aggregate liability under any Project Schedule shall not exceed the sum of retainer fees paid plus value share payments received under that Project Schedule. For Option B: Standard liability provisions apply." | The value-based model requires differentiated liability treatment. CBS Group accepts reduced payment where value is not delivered but cannot accept unlimited liability for outcome shortfalls in an advisory engagement. |

| 8 | New Schedule 3: Project Schedule Template | Not currently included | Add new schedule: "Schedule 3 - Project Schedule Template: Each Option A initiative shall be governed by a Project Schedule executed by both parties prior to commencement. The Project Schedule shall document: (a) Scope and deliverables; (b) Baseline costs or performance metrics; (c) Value measurement methodology aligned with CATALYST 7™; (d) Start date, end date, and measurement period; (e) Team composition and resource commitment; (f) Value share percentage and any applicable cap; (g) Validation tier and process per clause 12.5; (h) Governance and reporting requirements. A template Project Schedule is attached as Annexure [X]. Option B engagements shall be governed by Work Orders in accordance with standard terms." | Structured documentation for each initiative ensures clarity of scope, expectations, and commercial terms. The template approach provides efficiency whilst maintaining flexibility for initiative-specific requirements. |

| Capital | Value Creation Pathway |

| Manufactured | Enhanced asset performance through adoption of proven technologies; improved durability specifications |

| Intellectual | Process improvements embedded in standards; methodology transfer to TfNSW teams; innovation pathways enabled |

## Alt 2_CBSE VBP - FINAL DRAFT.docx

*File: `Working finals/Alternatives/Alt 2_CBSE VBP - FINAL DRAFT.docx`*

1st December 2025

David Hanna
Strategic Sourcing Manager
Transport for NSW
Level 2, 7 Harvest Street
Macquarie Park NSW 2113

Subject: Alternate Offer 2 for Asset Management Services

Dear David,

On behalf of CBSE, a Joint Venture between CBS Group and Egis, I am pleased to submit our alternate offer for the provision of Asset Management services to Transport for NSW. This proposal responds to our discussions regarding innovative commercial models and may be accepted either as an alternate to our fully compliant offer or as a supplemental engagement mechanism to meet TfNSW's asset management and value creation needs over the term.

The offer comprises a flexible dual-pathway commercial framework designed to foster a genuine partnership between our organisations:

Value Based Pricing

This pathway offers a shared-risk model that directly aligns our compensation with the tangible value we deliver. It moves beyond the conventional client-consultant relationship—where value risk sits entirely with the client—to a collaborative partnership focused on measurable outcomes.

Traditional Time-Based Engagement

This pathway provides for engagements with clearly defined scope, offering access to the full depth of expertise available from CBS Group and Egis through conventional fee arrangements.

Collaborative Contract Terms

Our fully compliant offer provides for 100% acceptance of the proposed contract terms. However, these terms pass risks to CBSE that come at a cost. We have assessed the value of terms representing additional risk and propose reduced fees where the amendments in this alternate offer are accepted.

This framework provides TfNSW with the flexibility to select the most appropriate commercial approach for each engagement, ensuring our incentives are always aligned with your objectives.

We are confident this framework offers a compelling value proposition: it combines the deep local expertise of CBS Group with the global capabilities of Egis, enabling us to accept risk on value-based pricing while providing additional service value in exchange for collaborative commercial risk allocation.

We have enclosed a detailed proposal outlining the framework, its governance, and the value it is designed to create.

Thank you for the opportunity to present this alternate offer. We look forward to discussing it with you further.

Sincerely,

Sarah Taylor
Partner, Strategy & Technology
CBS Group Pty Ltd
