Executive summary

Situation: Traditional models of consultant engagement leave all value risk with the client. Consultants are paid for their time and deliverables regardless of whether their recommendations generate tangible benefits. This creates a fundamental misalignment of incentives—the consultant's commercial success is disconnected from the client's actual outcomes. Similarly, standard contract terms allocate risks to the consultant that come at a cost, yet provide no corresponding benefit to the client when those risks do not materialise.

Complication: Value in complex infrastructure engagements is inherently uncertain. Even well-conceived initiatives may not deliver anticipated benefits due to implementation challenges, changing circumstances or unforeseen constraints. Under traditional arrangements, TfNSW bears this uncertainty entirely while consultants face no consequence for recommendations that fail to materialise as value. Meanwhile, contract terms that transfer risk to consultants simply result in higher fees to cover contingencies that may never eventuate—cost without benefit.

Response: CBSE proposes an alternate engagement model comprising a flexible dual-pathway commercial framework with three complementary mechanisms.

Method 1 - Traditional Time-Based Engagement provides access to the full depth of expertise available from CBS Group and Egis through conventional fee arrangements for engagements with clearly defined scope on Collaborative Contract Terms.  It provides for engagement of our core and supplementary teams on amended commercial terms. TfNSW acceptance of these terms allows a reduction in CBSE costs and risk premium, enabling reduced fees. This creates a direct exchange: TfNSW accepts manageable risks in return for tangible cost savings.

Method 2 - Value Based Pricing structures our compensation around shared risk and aligned returns, operating on three foundational principles: shared conviction (both parties agree there is genuine possibility of value before work commences), co-investment (CBSE assigns appropriate specialists on a fixed retainer basis, with value share agreed initiative-by-initiative), and aligned returns (if value is delivered, we share in it; if not, our returns are limited accordingly).

We bring a suite of proven asset management products, methodologies and analytical tools that have delivered more than $1 billion in validated savings for TfNSW over the past three years. Combined with the global capabilities of Egis, it is with this capability and track record that we are able to accept shared risk with confidence.

Mechanism 1 (Traditional Time-Based Engagement) and Mechanism 2 (Collaborative Contract Terms) are detailed at Section 4 of this Alternate Proposal. The remainder of this document explains the value and operation of Mechanism 3 (Value Based Pricing).

We look forward to working through our seed list of initiatives and the necessary commercial amendments to rapidly enable this model and start driving real value to support the Asset Financial Sustainability (AFS) targets set for the organisation.

Dual engagement pathway framework

Framework structure

This alternative framework is built on two distinct and complementary engagement pathways, allowing TfNSW to select the most suitable approach for any given initiative.

Method 1: Traditional, Time-Based Engagement

This pathway follows a conventional consultancy model. Services are provided at market rates for a defined scope of work. This option is suitable for projects where the scope and deliverables are clearly defined from the outset and where a traditional risk allocation is preferred. Services are provided at market rates for a defined scope of work. Work orders do not include value share provisions.

Method 2: Shared-Risk, Value-Based Engagement

This pathway is founded on a partnership model. CBSE accepts a significantly reduced monthly retainer, representing a substantial investment from our side. In return, we participate in the value we help create through a pre-agreed sharing mechanism on validated benefits. This model ensures that both organisations share in the risks and rewards, creating a strong alignment of interests.

Selection Criteria

The choice between Method 1 and Method 2 would be guided by the nature of the project.

Method 1 is the preferred choice for more straightforward engagements. This includes defined-scope studies, technical investigations, or when seeking short-term expert advice. It is also appropriate for engagements where a more conservative, traditional risk allocation is desired

Method 2 is particularly well-suited for initiatives with the potential for significant value creation, such as long-term transformation programs or projects that require innovative solutions. It is most effective in situations where a deep, collaborative partnership and fully aligned incentives are critical to success.

Flexibility and Combination

The framework is designed for flexibility, allowing TfNSW to:

Employ different options for different projects concurrently

Transition between the two options with an agreed-upon notice period

Combine both options within a single large-scale program

Initiate with a pilot of Method 1 before committing to a broader rollout

The Case for a New Engagement Model (Method 2)

The Problem with Traditional Consulting

The conventional consulting model contains a structural flaw that limits its effectiveness for infrastructure agencies. Under traditional fee-for-service arrangements, consultants are compensated for effort expended rather than value delivered.

This misalignment creates predictable consequences:

Infrastructure asset management initiatives involve inherent uncertainty. Even expertly designed interventions may not achieve intended outcomes due to organisational readiness, competing priorities, market conditions, or technical constraints that emerge during implementation. This uncertainty is not a failure of planning—it is a reality of complex systems.

The question is not whether to accept this uncertainty, but how to allocate it fairly between client and consultant. Under traditional models, TfNSW accepts all of this uncertainty while consultants face no consequence for recommendations that fail to materialise as value.

A Different Approach: Shared Risk, Aligned Returns

CBS Group proposes an alternative model structured around three foundational principles:

Shared conviction: Both parties agree there is genuine possibility of value in each initiative before work commences.

Co-investment:  CBSE JV assigns specialists on a retainer basis at 40-50% of market rates, with value, duration and value share agreed initiative-by-initiative.

Aligned returns: If value is delivered, we share in that value. If it is not, our returns are limited accordingly.

The following comparison illustrates how fundamentally this differs from traditional arrangements:

Risk and Reward Distribution

The model's value becomes clearer when examining how outcomes translate to returns:

Why CBSE JV Can Accept This Risk

Traditional consultants cannot easily adopt value-based models because they lack the tools to assess value potential with confidence.  CBSE JV's position is different.

Our willingness to share value risk is underpinned by a proven toolkit developed through successful delivery across major infrastructure projects including the Western Harbour Tunnel, Sydney Harbour Tunnel, and M6 Stage 1:

These capabilities do not eliminate uncertainty—they reduce it to a level where shared risk becomes commercially viable.

Partnership Foundation

This proposal builds upon CBS Group's established relationship with TfNSW, spanning over 20 years of engagement with NSW transport infrastructure. Our current integration of CBS specialists within TfNSW has demonstrated our critical thinking, pragmatic technical and commercial solutions, and ability to engage with a wide variety of stakeholders.

To enhance the credibility of our value-based model, we have established a strategic alliance with ST Engineering. Their role is to provide:

Independent validation of benefits claimed

Global benchmarking against international standards

Technology assessments based on worldwide deployments

Importantly, CBSE JV remains the sole contracting party—we manage all coordination with ST Engineering, maintaining a single interface for TfNSW throughout the engagement.

The Value-Based Engagement Model

Core principles

The value-based engagement model fundamentally restructures the consultant-client relationship around three principles that address the limitations of traditional arrangements:

Principle 1: Shared Conviction

Before any initiative commences, both TfNSW and CBSE JV must agree that a genuine possibility of value exists. This is not a formality—it requires both parties to review the proposed intervention, examine the evidence base, and commit resources only where there is mutual confidence in the potential for measurable improvement.

Principle 2: Co-Investment

 CBSE JV assigns a team of appropriate specialists to agreed initiatives on a retainer basis. The value target, task duration and value share percentage are agreed on an initiative-by-initiative basis.  CBSE JV's initial fees are set at 40-50% of market rates, representing our investment in the partnership. This reduced fee is not a discount—it reflects our willingness to accept a lower certain return in exchange for participation in value upside.

Principle 3: Aligned Returns

If value is delivered and independently validated,  CBSE JV shares in that value through benefit-sharing payments. If value does not materialise, our returns are limited to the reduced retainer. This structure ensures that  CBSE JV's commercial success depends directly on TfNSW's outcomes.

Process overview 

The value-based pricing model operates as illustrated in the diagram and incorporating the following steps:

Client or CBSE identifies a value-generating opportunity

CBSE and TfNSW agree on scope, baseline costs, method of measurement (considering CATALYST 7™), start and end date, number of teams, and value share (with cap if required)

A Project Schedule is completed and executed by both parties

Project commences with monthly retainers for the agreed number of teams paid by TfNSW

Value share is paid once measurement outcomes are confirmed as documented in the Project Schedule

Identification & Scoping

Either party may identify a potential value-generating opportunity.  CBSE JV will maintain an ongoing pipeline of opportunities through its embedded team and strategic analysis.

For each identified opportunity,  CBSE JV and TfNSW will jointly:

Define the problem statement and anticipated outcome

Establish baseline costs or performance metrics

Agree the measurement methodology aligned with CATALYST 7™

Determine the number of teams and resource commitment required

Agree the start date, end date, and value measurement period

Negotiate the value share percentage and any applicable cap

Agree Terms and Execute Schedule

The agreed terms will be documented in a Project Schedule, which forms a binding appendix to the Master Agreement. Work on the initiative will not commence until both parties have executed the Project Schedule.

Delivery

Upon execution of the Project Schedule:

Monthly retainers for the agreed number of teams commence

Baseline data is formally documented and certified

The project team is mobilised in accordance with the agreed composition

Realise Value 

Upon completion of the initiative:

Value is measured in accordance with the agreed methodology

Benefits are validated through the applicable validation tier

Value share is calculated and paid as documented in the Project Schedule

Project Schedule Requirements

Before work commences on any value-based initiative, both parties will agree and document the following in a Project Schedule (Appendix A):

Scope: The specific activities, deliverables and outcomes for the initiative

Baseline: The current state costs or performance metrics against which value will be measured

Measurement Method: The methodology for calculating value, aligned with the CATALYST 7™ framework

Duration: The start date, end date, and value measurement period

Team Composition: The number and composition of teams assigned to the initiative

Value Share: The percentage payable to  CBSE JV, including any applicable cap

Retainer Framework

The value-based model operates through initiative-specific retainers, with the number of teams determined for each Project Schedule based on the scope and complexity of the work.

Base Retainer Terms

Team Allocation

The number of teams assigned to each initiative will be agreed in the Project Schedule based on:

Scope and complexity of the initiative

Expected duration

Target value and delivery timeframes

Resource requirements identified during scoping

Multiple initiatives may run concurrently, each with its own team allocation and Project Schedule.

Minimum Deliverables Within Retainer

Each monthly retainer includes:

Monthly performance dashboard and benefit tracking report

Access to CATALYST 7™ tools and templates for engaged projects

Monthly stakeholder briefing and progress review

Continuous monitoring of asset performance against established baselines

Value Share Mechanism

Value share is calculated and paid on an initiative-by-initiative basis. For each value-generating opportunity,  CBSE JV and TfNSW will execute a Project Schedule that documents the specific commercial terms applicable to that initiative.

The value share payable for each initiative is calculated as follows:

Value Share = (Validated Benefits - Cumulative Retainer Paid) × Agreed Value Share Percentage

The value share is payable only after the measurement outcomes are confirmed through the validation process documented in the Project Schedule.

Commercial Framework

The commercial structure operationalises these principles through an integrated pricing arrangement:

This structure creates a partnership dynamic fundamentally different from traditional consulting.  CBSE JV has a direct financial interest in ensuring that initiatives succeed—not merely that deliverables are completed.

Validation Framework

Validation Principles

The validation process is governed by the following principles to ensure both rigour and efficiency:

Transparency: All benefit calculations and supporting evidence are shared with TfNSW

Conservatism: Where uncertainty exists, benefit estimates err on the conservative side

Attribution: Only benefits demonstrably linked to  CBSE JV interventions are included

Timing: Benefits are recognised when realised, not when projected

Independence: ST Engineering provides objective assessment at all tiers

Tiered Validation Approach

A robust validation framework is essential to the integrity of the value-based model. Both parties must have confidence that claimed benefits are real, measurable and attributable to the engagement. We propose a tiered validation approach that applies appropriate scrutiny proportionate to benefit magnitude.

The following table sets out the validation requirements at each tier (may be amended in the project schedule by agreement):

Benchmarking and Global Comparison

ST Engineering's involvement enables benefits to be contextualised against international standards. This benchmarking serves two purposes: it validates that claimed improvements are genuine by reference to what has been achieved elsewhere, and it identifies further opportunities by highlighting gaps between current performance and global best practice.

Initial (Seed) Initiatives for Consideration

Seed List of Value-Based Initiatives

 CBSE JV has developed an initial seed list of initiatives for consideration by TfNSW under the value-based engagement model. Each initiative has been assessed for value potential, implementation complexity and alignment with TfNSW strategic priorities. The value, duration and value share for each initiative would be agreed prior to commencement in accordance with the model principles.

The following table presents the proposed initiatives:

Initiative Selection and Prioritisation

The initiatives listed above represent opportunities identified through  CBSE JV's existing engagement with TfNSW and analysis of strategic priorities. Not all initiatives need proceed simultaneously—the value-based model allows for selective engagement based on TfNSW priorities and resource availability.

We propose the following approach to initiative selection:

Joint prioritisation workshop:  CBSE JV and TfNSW jointly review the seed list and agree priority initiatives

Value assessment: For each priority initiative, both parties review the value hypothesis and agree target outcomes

Commercial agreement: Value target, duration, and value share percentage are agreed for each initiative

Phased commencement: Initiatives commence in agreed sequence, allowing learnings to inform subsequent engagements

Traditional Engagement Under Collaborative Terms

To enable engagement of our core and supplementary teams at a reduced set of rates, the proposed amendments to the contract detailed at Appendix C Table 1 must be accepted.

To enable the value-based engagement model amendments to the contract detailed at Appendix C Table 2 must be accepted.

These amendments are designed to be applied to TfNSW's Professional Services Contract (Version 4.0, September 2024) as provided in Annexure A - Proposed Contract.

Engagement Process

Our Commitment

 CBSE JV commits to fostering a genuine partnership with shared success, delivering validated and measurable value, maintaining single-point accountability throughout the engagement, providing global insights with local delivery, and creating alignment of interests that benefits both parties.

This partnership proposal is valid for 90 days and represents  CBSE JV's commitment to deliver world-class, validated asset management solutions through a commercial model that ensures our success depends on yours.

Appendix E: Catalyst7TM Measurement Framework

 CBSE JV Principles for Using CATALYST 7™ and Applying Value-Based Pricing

Overview

CATALYST 7™ is CBS Group's proprietary framework for delivering and measuring value across infrastructure advisory engagements. Developed through over a decade of application and more than $1 billion in documented savings for Transport for NSW and other major infrastructure clients, CATALYST 7™ provides a comprehensive methodology that moves beyond traditional time-and-materials consulting to create strategic partnerships where compensation is directly tied to measurable value creation.

The framework recognises that infrastructure value extends far beyond financial returns to encompass seven interconnected capital dimensions, enabling CBS Group to identify, quantify, and deliver holistic value that benefits all stakeholders.

The Seven Capitals Framework

CATALYST 7™ measures and optimises value across seven interconnected capital dimensions:

Value Multiplication Principles

CATALYST 7™ recognises that the seven capitals form an interconnected system where interventions in one dimension create ripple effects across others. CBS Group actively designs interventions to maximise synergistic effects through three mechanisms:

Cross-Capital Synergies: Interventions in one capital dimension enhance value creation in another (e.g., Digital Capital investments enhance Manufactured Capital performance while improving Natural Capital outcomes).

Cascading Value Effects: Value created in one dimension triggers sequential value creation in others through mapped pathways.

Reinforcing Feedback Loops: Value creation across multiple dimensions establishes self-reinforcing cycles that compound over time.

Typical value multipliers range from 1.8x–2.7x for two-dimensional interactions, 2.5x–3.8x for three-dimensional synergies, and 3.2x–4.7x for comprehensive multi-dimensional integration.

Value-Based Pricing Commercial Model

Dual-Component Structure

CBS Group's VBP model comprises two components designed to balance budget predictability with strong performance incentives:

Strategic Partnership Retainer: Fixed monthly fee covering strategic advisory, methodology development, knowledge transfer, governance support, and continuous improvement activities.

Value-Based Success Fees: Calculated as a percentage of independently validated value creation across the seven capitals, typically representing 60–80% of total CBS compensation in successful engagements.

Measurement and Validation

CATALYST 7™ applies rigorous measurement protocols to ensure credible value creation claims:

Baseline Rigor: Comprehensive baseline assessment incorporating 3–5 years of historical performance data, industry benchmarks, and explicit counterfactual analysis.

Conservative Estimation: Lower-bound estimates, time-value discounting, and exclusion of weakly attributed value categories.

Independent Validation: All material value claims (>$1M) undergo third-party validation by qualified assessors, with full methodology and data transparency.

Performance Guarantees

CBS Group typically guarantees minimum value creation of 3x–5x total retainer fees over the engagement period, demonstrating confidence in the CATALYST 7™ methodology. Remediation mechanisms include success fee waivers, partial retainer refunds, and extended engagement commitments if guaranteed minimums are not achieved.

Perfect Incentive Alignment

CATALYST 7™ VBP fundamentally restructures the consultant-client relationship:

Outcome-Based Compensation: CBS Group's compensation is calculated as a percentage of documented value creation, ensuring CBS earns more when clients achieve more value.

Risk-Sharing: CBS Group bears significant performance risk—if interventions fail to create value, CBS receives only the base retainer without success fees.

Long-Term Partnership: Engagements typically span 3–5 years with phased success fee payments tied to sustained value creation.

Capability Transfer: Structured knowledge transfer builds client capabilities over time, enabling increasingly independent value creation.

Summary

CATALYST 7™ represents a fundamental shift from traditional consulting to genuine strategic partnership. By measuring and optimising value across seven interconnected capitals, applying rigorous baseline and validation protocols, and structuring commercial arrangements around shared risk and reward, CBS Group delivers transformative outcomes where consultant success depends entirely on delivering measurable, sustainable client value.

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| No. | Topic | Proposed Amendment | Reason | Category |

| 1 | Intellectual Property – Pre-existing IP (Clause 4.1) | Amend clause 4.1(c) to read: "The Contractor grants to the Principal a non-exclusive, irrevocable, perpetual, transferable, royalty-free licence to use, reproduce, communicate, and modify or incorporate into other works (and sub-licence any third party to do so) the Contractor's Pre-existing IPR to the extent incorporated in the Deliverables as required by the Principal to receive, use and otherwise enjoy the full benefit of the Services." | The current drafting grants TfNSW an unrestricted licence to all  CBSE JV pre-existing IP used in connection with the engagement, rather than limiting the licence to IP actually incorporated in deliverables. This could inadvertently transfer rights to  CBSE JV's frameworks and methodology, proprietary asset management tools, and analytical models that are merely used in performing services but not delivered. The proposed amendment preserves TfNSW's full benefit of deliverables whilst protecting  CBSE JV's core IP. | IP Protection |

| 2 | Intellectual Property – New IP Ownership (Clause 4.2) | Add new sub-clause 4.2(c): "Notwithstanding clause 4.2(a), where the Contractor creates methodologies, frameworks, tools, templates or know-how of general application that are not specific to the Principal's requirements (General Methodologies), such General Methodologies shall remain the property of the Contractor, with the Principal granted a perpetual, non-exclusive, royalty-free licence to use such General Methodologies for NSW transport purposes." | The blanket assignment of all New IPR to TfNSW would capture improvements  CBSE JV makes to its frameworks and other proprietary methodologies during the engagement. Asset management consulting inherently involves continuous methodology refinement. The proposed amendment distinguishes between client-specific deliverables (which should vest in TfNSW) and general methodological improvements (which should remain with  CBSE JV). | IP Protection |

| 3 | Limitation of Liability – Exclusions (Clause 12.2) | Amend clause 12.2(a)(ii) to read: "liability under clause 4 (Intellectual Property Rights) for which the Contractor's aggregate liability shall be limited to 200% of fees paid under this Contract , 12.3 (Indemnities), 13 (Privacy and protection of Principal Data), 14 (Confidentiality) or 17 (Work health and safety and Workplace Laws);" | The current drafting carves out IP liability entirely from the liability cap. Given the broad IP assignment provisions and the nature of professional advisory services (where IP issues may arise from good faith disagreements over ownership), unlimited IP liability creates disproportionate exposure. The proposed sub-cap provides meaningful protection for TfNSW whilst capping  CBSE JV's maximum exposure. | Risk Allocation |

| 4 | Indemnities – Proportionality (Clause 12.3) | Amend opening words of clause 12.3 to read: "Subject to clause 12.4, the Contractor indemnifies..." And insert new clause 12.3(f): "The indemnities in clauses 12.3(a) to (e) are subject to the limitations in clause 12.1 except to the extent arising from the Contractor's fraud, wilful misconduct, or criminal conduct." | The current indemnity provisions are uncapped and fall outside the liability limitation. For professional services, uncapped indemnities are inconsistent with insurability and standard market practice. The proposed amendment subjects indemnities to the overall liability cap whilst carving out egregious conduct, providing proportionate protection for both parties. | Risk Allocation |

| 5 | Termination for Convenience – Payment (Clause 10.4) | Amend clause 10.4(b)(iii) to add sub-paragraph (C): "(C) a termination payment equal to [10]% of the fees that would have been payable for Services in the three months following termination, being compensation for the Contractor's costs of repositioning resources and lost opportunity." | The current termination for convenience provisions entitle  CBSE JV only to payment for work completed and authorised third-party costs. For an engagement requiring dedicated specialist resources, sudden termination causes material harm through stranded capacity and lost opportunity costs. The proposed amendment provides modest compensation reflecting market practice for specialist engagements whilst preserving TfNSW's termination right. | Commercial Terms |

| 6 | Insurance – Professional Indemnity Run-off (Clause 5.1) | Amend clause 5.1(b) to read: "professional indemnity insurance specified in the Key Details, such insurance to be maintained until the expiry of 3 years following completion of the relevant Contractor's Activities;" | The current requirement for 6 years' run-off cover exceeds market standard for professional services of this duration and scope. Given the advisory nature of the engagement and typical policy availability, 3 years provides adequate protection aligned with limitation periods for professional negligence whilst being commercially practical for a small consulting practice. | Insurance |


| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| No. | Clause Reference | Current Provision | Proposed Amendment | Rationale |

| 1 | 2.1 Term | Standard fixed term with option periods | Add: "For engagements, the initial term shall be 2 years with performance gates at 6, 12 and 18 months. Subject to achieving agreed performance metrics, the term may be extended for 2 x 2-year periods. Performance gates will assess: value delivered, milestone achievement, and stakeholder satisfaction. Either party may exit at performance gates if minimum thresholds are not met." | Value-based engagements require time for benefits to materialise. Performance gates provide TfNSW with structured exit opportunities whilst enabling  CBSE JV to demonstrate value over an appropriate horizon. |

| 2 | 9.1 All work included | Fixed price includes all work | Replace with: "For Method 1 (Value-Based): Monthly retainer covers base services. Value share payments calculated separately based on validated benefits. For Method 2 (Traditional): Standard all-inclusive pricing applies." | The dual-pathway model requires distinct treatment of value-based retainer/share payments and traditional fixed pricing to operate effectively. |

| 3 | 9.2 Payment | Standard payment terms | Add: "Method 1 payments: (a) Monthly retainer paid in advance by 1st business day; (b) Value share paid within 30 days of benefit validation. Method 2: Standard 30-day payment terms." | Clear payment timing is essential for cash flow certainty under the value-based model, recognising the different nature of retainer and performance-based payments. |

| 4 | 9.3 Invoices | Standard invoice requirements | Add: "Method 1 value share invoices must include: benefit calculation methodology, validation evidence, and independent verification (where required under clause 12.5)." | Transparency and auditability of value share calculations is essential for both parties' confidence in the model. |

| 5 | New Clause 12.5 Value Measurement | Not currently addressed | Add new clause: "12.5 Value Measurement: Parties agree to the CATALYST 7™ methodology for benefit calculation using pre-agreed templates. Streamlined validation thresholds apply: (a) <$100k: self-certification; (b) $100k-$250k: expedited peer review (5 business days); (c) $250k-$500k: standard peer review; (d) >$500k: independent validation. Templates for common benefit types will be agreed at commencement." | Efficient validation processes are essential for model operation. Tiered thresholds reduce administrative burden for smaller initiatives whilst maintaining rigour for larger value claims. |

| 6 | New Clause 12.6 Governance Structure | Not currently addressed | Add new clause: "12.6 Partnership Governance: A Joint Steering Committee shall be established with equal representation from both parties. The Committee shall conduct monthly operational reviews, quarterly value assessments, and annual partnership evaluations. Decisions regarding initiative prioritisation, value validation disputes, and framework modifications shall be made by the Committee." | Partnership governance provides TfNSW with visibility and shared control over the engagement, supporting collaborative value delivery and dispute prevention. |

| 7 | 12.1 Liability | Standard liability provisions | Add: "For Method 1 engagements: (a) Liability for retainer services is capped at the annual retainer paid under the relevant Project Schedule; (b) The Contractor bears no liability for value share amounts where validated benefits are not achieved, provided the Contractor has applied its methodology in accordance with Good Industry Practice; (c) The Contractor's aggregate liability under any Project Schedule shall not exceed the sum of retainer fees paid plus value share payments received under that Project Schedule. For Method 2: Standard liability provisions apply." | The value-based model requires differentiated liability treatment.  CBSE JV accepts reduced payment where value is not delivered but cannot accept unlimited liability for outcome shortfalls in an advisory engagement. |

| 8 | New Schedule 3: Project Schedule Template | Not currently included | Add new schedule: "Schedule 3 - Project Schedule Template: Each Method 1 initiative shall be governed by a Project Schedule executed by both parties prior to commencement. The Project Schedule shall document: (a) Scope and deliverables; (b) Baseline costs or performance metrics; (c) Value measurement methodology aligned with CATALYST 7™; (d) Start date, end date, and measurement period; (e) Team composition and resource commitment; (f) Value share percentage and any applicable cap; (g) Validation tier and process per clause 12.5; (h) Governance and reporting requirements. A template Project Schedule is attached as Annexure [X]. Method 2 engagements shall be governed by Work Orders in accordance with standard terms." | Structured documentation for each initiative ensures clarity of scope, expectations, and commercial terms. The template approach provides efficiency whilst maintaining flexibility for initiative-specific requirements. |

## Alt 3_CBS Products VBP - FINAL DRAFT.docx

*File: `Working finals/Alternatives/Alt 3_CBS Products VBP - FINAL DRAFT.docx`*

1st December 2025

David Hanna
Strategic Sourcing Manager
Transport for NSW
Level 2, 7 Harvest Street
Macquarie Park NSW 2113

Subject: Alternate Offer 3 for Asset Management Services

Dear David,

On behalf of CBS Group, I am pleased to submit our alternate offer for the provision of Asset Management services to Transport for NSW. This proposal responds to our discussions regarding access to specialist products and is designed to operate in parallel with either our conforming base offer or Alternate 1 offer, providing a supplemental engagement mechanism to meet TfNSW's asset management and value creation needs over the term.

The offer provides TfNSW with access to a suite of CBS Group specialist products developed through our extensive experience delivering asset management outcomes for major infrastructure. These products represent proven methodologies, analytical tools and frameworks that have contributed to more than $1 billion in validated savings for TfNSW over the past three years.

Product Licensing

Each product may be acquired as a perpetual licence, providing TfNSW with ongoing access and the ability to deploy the product independently across the organisation. This pathway suits products where TfNSW seeks long-term capability uplift and internal ownership.

Product as a Service

Alternatively, products may be accessed on a service basis comprising an implementation fee and monthly usage charges. This pathway provides flexibility for time-limited initiatives, pilot programs or where TfNSW prefers operational expenditure over capital investment.

Collaborative Contract Terms

To accommodate the product licensing and usage mechanisms required, we have proposed a set of amendments to the contract terms. These amendments address intellectual property provisions, licensing arrangements and service-based commercial structures not contemplated in the standard terms. Acceptance of these amendments enables the product framework to operate effectively alongside the primary engagement.

This framework provides TfNSW with the flexibility to select the most appropriate commercial approach for each product, enabling rapid deployment of proven tools to support asset management objectives.

We have enclosed a detailed proposal outlining a selection of current products, licensing options, proposed contract amendments, and the value each product is designed to create.  Thank you for the opportunity to present this alternate offer. We look forward to discussing it with you further.

Sincerely,

Sarah Taylor, 
Partner, Strategy and Technology
CBS Group Pty Ltd

Table of Contents

Executive summary	1

1.	Product Portfolio	1

1.1.	Selected products summary	1

1.2.	Initiative Selection and Prioritisation	3

2.	Next Steps	3

2.1.	Engagement Process	3

 Appendix A: Proposed Contract Amendments	4

A.1. Amendments Common to Both Pathways	5

A.2. Amendments Specific to Perpetual Licence Pathway	6

A.3. Amendments Specific to Product as a Service (PaaS) Pathway	7

Executive summary

Situation: Delivering effective asset management for complex infrastructure requires sophisticated tools, methodologies and analytical frameworks. Developing these capabilities internally demands significant investment in time, expertise and iterative refinement. Traditional consultant engagements provide access to such tools on a project-by-project basis, but the intellectual property remains with the consultant—requiring repeated engagement to access the same capabilities.

Complication: TfNSW faces growing pressure to deliver asset management outcomes efficiently while building sustainable internal capability. Reliance on consultant-held tools creates dependency and limits knowledge transfer. Procuring or developing equivalent products independently would require substantial investment with uncertain outcomes. Standard contract terms do not contemplate product licensing or service-based access models, creating a structural barrier to accessing proven tools through mechanisms suited to their nature.

Response: CBS Group proposes an alternate engagement model designed to operate in parallel with either the conforming base offer or Alternate 1 offer. This model provides access to our suite of specialist products through a flexible dual-pathway commercial framework with three complementary mechanisms.

Product Licensing provides perpetual access to CBS Group products, enabling TfNSW to deploy proven tools independently across the organisation. This pathway supports long-term capability uplift and internal ownership of critical asset management methodologies.

Product as a Service provides access through an implementation fee and monthly usage charges. This pathway offers flexibility for time-limited initiatives, pilot programs or where operational expenditure is preferred over capital investment. It includes ongoing support and updates for the duration of the service arrangement.

Contract Amendments for Product Access details a set of proposed amendments to accommodate the licensing and usage mechanisms required. These amendments address intellectual property provisions, licensing arrangements and service-based commercial structures not contemplated in the standard terms, enabling the product framework to operate effectively alongside the primary engagement.

We bring a suite of proven asset management products, methodologies and analytical tools that have delivered more than $1 billion in validated savings for TfNSW over the past three years. It is with this track record that we offer these products with confidence in the value they will deliver.

Product Portfolio

Selected products summary

The following table provides a list of currently identified value based proposals that may be immediately activated. This framework may be applied to these and other opportunities that may arise during the term addressing TfNSW priorities:

Table 1: Selected Products

Initiative Selection and Prioritisation

The initiatives listed above represent opportunities identified through CBS Group's existing engagement with TfNSW and analysis of strategic priorities. Not all initiatives need proceed simultaneously—the value-based model allows for selective engagement based on TfNSW priorities and resource availability.

We propose the following approach to initiative selection:

Joint prioritisation workshop: CBS Group and TfNSW jointly review the seed list and agree priority initiatives

Value assessment: For each priority initiative, both parties review the value hypothesis and agree target outcomes

Commercial agreement: Value target, duration, and value share percentage are agreed for each initiative

Phased commencement: Initiatives commence in agreed sequence, allowing learnings to inform subsequent engagements

Next Steps

Engagement Process

We propose the following progression to establish the products framework:

Table Alt3 2: Engagement Process

Appendix A: Proposed Contract Amendments

To implement this innovative framework within TfNSW's standard Professional Services Contract, we propose the following amendments to accommodate the procurement pathways.

The proposed contract amendments required to enable the CBS Group Products Offer (Alternate 3) to operate alongside the primary engagement. The Products Offer provides TfNSW with access to CBS Group's specialist products through two commercial pathways:

Perpetual Licence: One-time acquisition providing TfNSW with ongoing access and independent deployment capability across the organisation.

Product as a Service (PaaS): Implementation fee plus recurring usage-based fees, providing flexibility for time-limited or pilot deployments with ongoing support and updates.

| Clause | Current Provision | Proposed Amendment | Rationale |

| 1.1 Definitions | Standard service definitions | Add definitions for: 'Product', 'Licence', 'Product as a Service', 'Product Fees', 'Product IP', 'Product Schedule', 'SLA', 'Usage Metrics', 'Usage Period' | Establishes terminology required for product-based commercial arrangements distinct from traditional services |

| New Clause 4.9 Product Arrangements | Not addressed | Add clause: (a) CBS Group may provide Products under this Contract via Licence or PaaS as specified in a Product Schedule. (b) Each Product Schedule forms part of this Contract. (c) Product IP remains with CBS Group. (d) The Principal receives rights specified in the Product Schedule only. | Creates contractual mechanism for product delivery parallel to service delivery, maintaining IP ownership clarity |

| 8.1 Intellectual Property | IP in deliverables vests in Principal | Add clause 8.1(d): Product IP is excluded from clause 8.1(a)-(c). All Product IP, including enhancements developed during the engagement, remains the property of CBS Group. Principal receives licence rights as specified in the Product Schedule. | Protects CBS Group's product investment while providing Principal with clear usage rights |

| 9.2 Payment | Payment for services | Add clause 9.2(f): Product Fees are payable as specified in the relevant Product Schedule, separately from and in addition to any Service Fees. | Creates separate payment stream for product arrangements without affecting service fee provisions |

| 12.1 Liability | Standard liability provisions | Add clause 12.1(c): For Product arrangements, CBS Group's aggregate liability is limited to: (i) for Licence arrangements, the Licence Fee paid; (ii) for PaaS arrangements, twelve months of Usage Fees. | Product-specific liability cap reflects nature of product arrangements vs bespoke service delivery |

| New Schedule: Product Schedule | Not addressed | Add Product Schedule template containing: (a) Product identification and description; (b) Commercial pathway (Licence or PaaS); (c) Fees and payment terms; (d) Licence scope or Usage Metrics; (e) SLA provisions; (f) Support arrangements; (g) Term and termination. | Standardised template enables efficient product deployment while ensuring commercial clarity |


| --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Clause | Current Provision | Proposed Amendment | Rationale |

| New Clause 4.9(e) Licence Grant | Not addressed | Add clause: Upon payment of the Licence Fee, CBS Group grants the Principal a perpetual, non-exclusive, non-transferable licence to use the Product for internal business purposes within the NSW Government. The licence includes deployment across TfNSW and related NSW Government agencies as specified in the Product Schedule. | Defines scope of perpetual rights while protecting CBS Group's ability to licence to other parties |

| New Clause 4.9(f) Maintenance | Not addressed | Add clause: (a) Licence Fee includes 12 months of maintenance and support. (b) Ongoing maintenance may be purchased at 18% of Licence Fee per annum. (c) Maintenance includes bug fixes, security updates and minor enhancements. (d) Major version upgrades may be purchased separately. | Standard software maintenance model providing ongoing support option for perpetual licence holders |

| New Clause 4.9(g) Escrow | Not addressed | Add clause: For Licence arrangements exceeding $100,000, CBS Group will place source code in escrow with a mutually agreed escrow agent. Release conditions include CBS Group insolvency, material breach, or discontinuation of product support. | Provides Principal with business continuity protection for significant licence investments |


| --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Clause | Current Provision | Proposed Amendment | Rationale |

| New Clause 4.9(h) Usage Measurement | Not addressed | Add clause: (a) Usage will be measured in accordance with the Usage Metrics specified in the Product Schedule. (b) CBS Group will provide monthly usage reports. (c) Principal may request usage audit once per year at Principal's cost, unless audit reveals overcharge >5%, in which case CBS Group bears cost and refunds overcharge. | Establishes transparent usage measurement with fair audit mechanisms |

| 9.2 Payment (PaaS) | Standard service payment terms | Add clause 9.2(g): For PaaS arrangements: (i) implementation fees payable upon completion or in instalments as specified; (ii) usage fees payable monthly in arrears within 30 days; (iii) Principal must pay any Minimum Commitment regardless of actual usage; (iv) annual fee adjustments per Product Schedule. | PaaS arrangements require specific payment mechanics for recurring fees |

| New Clause 11.4 Service Credits | Not addressed | Add clause: (a) If CBS Group fails to meet SLA availability targets, Principal may claim service credits per Product Schedule. (b) Service credits are sole remedy for availability failures except material breach. (c) Credits applied to future invoices, not redeemable for cash. (d) Exclusions: scheduled maintenance, Principal-caused failures, force majeure. | Standard SaaS service credit model providing proportionate remedy for availability issues |

| New Clause 21.17 Data Portability | Not addressed | Add clause: (a) Upon termination or reasonable request, CBS Group will provide Principal's data in standard machine-readable format. (b) Data retained 90 days post-termination for export, then securely deleted. (c) Initial export at no charge; additional exports may be charged per Product Schedule. | Essential protection avoiding lock-in and enabling transition to alternative solutions |

| Product Schedule – PaaS Addendum | Not addressed | Add PaaS-specific Product Schedule sections: (a) SLA with availability targets (e.g., 99.5% uptime), measurement methodology, service credits; (b) Usage Metrics; (c) Minimum Commitments and overage rates; (d) Implementation scope and timeline; (e) Support services; (f) Data handling provisions; (g) Acceptable use policy; (h) Fee escalation provisions. | Comprehensive documentation of all PaaS-specific terms ensuring clear expectations |

the fully complaint WS5364262133 RFT response.

partnership—one that combines deep strategic insight with world-class delivery expertise.

brings its proven, data-driven methodologies for value-based pricing, strategic advisory, and complex

transparency.

## Alt 2_CBSE VBP - UPDATED FORMAT.docx

*File: `Working finals/Alternatives/Archive/Alt 2_CBSE VBP - UPDATED FORMAT.docx`*

1st December 2025

Carl Groom
Director Strategic Asset Management
Transport for NSW
Level 2, 7 Harvest Street 
Macquarie Park NSW 2113

Subject: Alternate Offer for Asset Management Services

Dear Carl, 

On behalf of CBSE, a Joint Venture between CBS Group and Egis, I am pleased to submit our alternate offer for the provision of Asset Management services to Transport for NSW. This proposal has been developed in response to our discussions regarding innovative commercial models and is submitted as part of our fully compliant offer.

This offer may be accepted as an alternate, or as a supplemental engagement mechanism to meet TfNSW Asset Management and Value Creation needs over the term.

The proposal introduces a flexible dual-pathway commercial framework designed to foster a genuine partnership between our organizations. This framework offers two distinct engagement options:

A shared-risk, value-based model that directly aligns our compensation with the tangible value we deliver to TfNSW.

A traditional, time-based model for engagements with a clearly defined scope and providing access to the full depth of expertise available from CBS Group and Egis.

This framework provides TfNSW with the flexibility to select the most appropriate commercial approach for each project, ensuring that our incentives are always aligned with your objectives. It is designed to move beyond the conventional client-consultant relationship to a collaborative partnership focused on delivering measurable outcomes.

We are confident that this alternate framework offers a compelling value proposition, combining the deep local expertise of CBS Group with the global capabilities of Egis. We have enclosed a detailed proposal that outlines the framework, its governance, and the value it is designed to create.

Thank you for the opportunity to present this alternate offer. We look forward to discussing it with you further.

Sincerely,

Table of Contents

Executive summary	1

1.	Introduction and context	2

1.1.	Purpose of this Alternative Proposal	2

1.2.	Relationship to our conforming offer	2

1.3.	CBSE capability overview	2

2.	Duel engagement pathway framework	4

2.1.	Framework structure	4

3.	Option A - shared risk, value-based engagement	5

3.2.	Value Share Mechanism	5

3.3.	Value Categories	5

4.1.	Traditional engagement pricing	8

4.2.	Traditional Engagement Terms	8

5.2.	Progressive Engagement Example	9

5.3.	Parallel Engagement Example	9

6.1.	Enabling the Value-Based Model	10

6.2.	Intellectual Property Protection	10

7.1.	Joint governance structure	13

7.2.	Implementation roadmap	13

7.3.	Risk management approach	13

8.	Why choose this alternative framework	15

8.1.	Key Framework Enhancements	15

8.2.	Innovation with options	15

8.3.	A proven partnership	15

9.	Next steps	17

9.1.	Proposed way forward	17

9.2.	Anticipated Outcomes	17

Executive summary

Situation

Traditional consulting models incentivise activity over outcomes. Consultants are compensated for time expended and deliverables completed, regardless of whether their recommendations generate tangible benefits. This structural misalignment means the client bears all value risk—paying full fees even when initiatives fail to deliver anticipated improvements.

Complication

Infrastructure asset management encompasses diverse work types with varying risk profiles and value certainty. Some initiatives are exploratory, with outcomes that cannot be guaranteed but offer significant potential value. Others have well-defined scope where deliverables and effort can be reliably estimated. A single engagement model cannot optimally address both situations: applying shared-risk pricing to defined-scope work undervalues consultant expertise, while applying fixed fees to uncertain-outcome work places disproportionate risk on the client.

Question

How can TfNSW engage specialist consultants through a framework that matches engagement models to work characteristics—sharing risk where value is uncertain, while providing cost certainty for defined-scope assignments?

Answer

CBSE—a joint venture combining CBS Group's deep TfNSW integration and proven CAPITAL framework with Egis's global infrastructure expertise—proposes a dual-pathway engagement framework offering genuine flexibility.

The framework provides two complementary options:

Option A: Shared-Risk, Value-Based Partnership — Below-market retainers with value sharing where pre-agreed benefits are realised. Both parties agree on value potential before work commences, co-invest in pursuing that value, and share in the outcomes achieved.

Option B: Traditional Time-Based Engagement — Market rates for defined-scope work, with upper limiting fees agreed with TfNSW. This pathway provides cost certainty for assignments where scope is clear and outcomes are predictable.

This flexibility allows TfNSW to select the most appropriate model for each project based on its specific objectives and risk appetite. CBSE brings the analytical tools, proven methodologies, and global benchmarking capability to accept shared risk with confidence—while offering competitive traditional pricing where that model better serves TfNSW's needs.

We look forward to working through the our seed list of initiatives and the necessary commercial amendments to rapidly enable this model and start driving real value to support the Asset Financial Sustainability (AFS) targets set for the organisation.

Introduction and context

Purpose of this Alternative Proposal

This document outlines an alternative commercial framework to our base proposal of October 2025. In response to our discussions with Transport for NSW concerning innovative commercial models and value-based procurement, we have developed a dual-pathway framework. This framework is designed to cater to the varied requirements of TfNSW's infrastructure portfolio by offering greater flexibility and alignment of interests.

We note that CBS Group has also submitted an alternate proposal for Option A engagement supported by ST Engineering, which follows the same commercial framework presented here. This CBSE proposal offers the added benefit of a blended team combining CBS Group's local expertise with Egis's global engineering capabilities, providing TfNSW with enhanced technical depth and international best practices while maintaining the same innovative commercial structure.

The proposed framework provides several enhancements compared to traditional consulting arrangements:

Commercial Flexibility: offering two distinct engagement options that can be selected based on project characteristics

Risk Sharing: includes options for shared risk, fostering a partnership through mutual financial commitment

Incentive Alignment: designed to ensure that both parties are rewarded for successful outcomes

Choice of Model: allows TfNSW to choose between an innovative, value-based model and a traditional, time-based model, depending on the specific needs and risk profile of the engagement

Relationship to our conforming offer

Our base proposal offers access to our full range of services under a conventional consulting agreement, which typically includes:

Time and materials engagement with fixed hourly or daily rates

Compensation based on time spent, regardless of the outcomes delivered

Traditional risk allocation, with the client bearing the implementation and value realisation risk

A focus on deliverables such as reports and recommendations, rather than on the achievement of specific outcomes

This alternative proposal introduces a different approach to the engagement model:

A shared-risk, value-based option where CBSE reduces its standard rates in exchange for a share in the value created

Performance-linked compensation that directly ties our fees to the validated benefits achieved by TfNSW

Mutual accountability mechanisms that require active participation from both parties to ensure success

Flexible engagement choices that allow for the selection of the most appropriate model for each type of project

CBSE capability overview

CBSE is a joint venture that brings together the local expertise of CBS Group and the global resources of Egis. This combination allows us to offer a unique blend of local knowledge and international best practices.

CBS Group: Has served TfNSW for over 20 years, delivering more than $1 billion in validated savings through its proprietary CATALYST 7™ methodology

Egis: Is an international engineering firm with 18,000 professionals worldwide and a strong focus on innovation

This CBSE proposal complements the CBS Group/ST Engineering alternate proposal by offering TfNSW a choice between:

CBS/ST Engineering Option: Focusing on CBS Group's core capabilities enhanced by ST Engineering's infrastructure and technology expertise

CBSE Option (this proposal): Providing a blended CBS-Egis team with integrated global engineering depth and European best practices

Both proposals utilise the same innovative commercial framework, ensuring TfNSW can select the team composition that best suits specific project requirements while maintaining consistent commercial terms.

Duel engagement pathway framework

Framework structure

This alternative framework is built on two distinct and complementary engagement pathways, allowing TfNSW to select the most suitable approach for any given initiative.

Option A: Shared-Risk, Value-Based Engagement

This pathway is founded on a partnership model. CBSE accepts a significantly reduced monthly retainer, representing a substantial investment from our side. In return, we participate in the value we help create through a pre-agreed sharing mechanism on validated benefits. This model ensures that both organisations share in the risks and rewards, creating a strong alignment of interests.

Option B: Traditional, Time-Based Engagement

This pathway follows a conventional consultancy model. Services are provided at market rates for a defined scope of work. This option is suitable for projects where the scope and deliverables are clearly defined from the outset and where a traditional risk allocation is preferred. Services are provided at market rates for a defined scope of work, with fixed price or time and materials options available. Work orders do not include value share provisions.

2.2 Selection Criteria

The choice between Option A and Option B would be guided by the nature of the project.

Option A is particularly well-suited for initiatives with the potential for significant value creation, such as long-term transformation programs or projects that require innovative solutions. It is most effective in situations where a deep, collaborative partnership and fully aligned incentives are critical to success.

Option B is the preferred choice for more straightforward engagements. This includes defined-scope studies, technical investigations, or when seeking short-term expert advice. It is also appropriate for engagements where a more conservative, traditional risk allocation is desired.

2.3 Flexibility and Combination

The framework is designed for flexibility, allowing TfNSW to:

Employ different options for different projects concurrently

Transition between the two options with an agreed-upon notice period

Combine both options within a single large-scale program

Initiate with a pilot of Option A before committing to a broader rollout

Option A - shared risk, value-based engagement

Retainer structure

The shared-risk model is founded on a retainer structure designed to create mutual commitment while providing certainty of minimum service levels. The base retainer for this model is set at a level significantly below the typical market rate for a comparable team, reflecting a joint investment in the success of the engagement.

Base Retainer Terms:

Fixed Monthly Amount: $10,000 per team (with a maximum of three specialists per team)

Payment Schedule: Monthly in advance, by the first business day of the month

Non-Refundable Nature: Payments are guaranteed to CBSE, irrespective of the value achieved, to ensure mutual commitment

Team Composition: A typical team includes a senior lead, a technical specialist, and an analyst

Availability Commitment: A minimum of 15 working days per month will be dedicated to TfNSW

Minimum Deliverables Within Retainer:

To provide certainty and demonstrate ongoing value, each monthly retainer includes:

Monthly performance dashboard and benefit tracking report

Quarterly opportunity assessment identifying new value creation possibilities

Access to CATALYST 7™ tools and templates for TfNSW use on engaged projects

Monthly stakeholder briefing and strategic planning session

Continuous monitoring of asset performance against established baselines

This retainer structure represents a significant investment from CBSE, as it forgoes a substantial portion of standard market revenue. This arrangement ensures that our focus is on generating value that will lead to a successful partnership, rather than on maximising billable hours.

Base Retainer Terms

3.1.2 Team Allocation

The number of teams assigned to each initiative will be agreed in the Project Schedule based on:

Scope and complexity of the initiative

Expected duration

Target value and delivery timeframes

Resource requirements identified during scoping

Multiple initiatives may run concurrently, each with its own team allocation and Project Schedule.

3.1.3 Minimum Deliverables Within Retainer

Each monthly retainer includes:

Monthly performance dashboard and benefit tracking report

Access to CATALYST 7™ tools and templates for engaged projects

Monthly stakeholder briefing and progress review

Continuous monitoring of asset performance against established baselines

Value Share Mechanism

Value share is calculated and paid on an initiative-by-initiative basis. For each value-generating opportunity, CBS Group and TfNSW will execute a Project Schedule that documents the specific commercial terms applicable to that initiative.

Project Schedule Requirements

Before work commences on any value-based initiative, both parties will agree and document the following in a Project Schedule (Appendix A):

Scope: The specific activities, deliverables and outcomes for the initiative

Baseline: The current state costs or performance metrics against which value will be measured

Measurement Method: The methodology for calculating value, aligned with the CATALYST 7™ framework

Duration: The start date, end date, and value measurement period

Team Composition: The number and composition of teams assigned to the initiative

Value Share: The percentage payable to CBS Group, including any applicable cap

Value Share Calculation

The value share payable for each initiative is calculated as follows:

Value Share = (Validated Benefits - Cumulative Retainer Paid) × Agreed Value Share Percentage

The value share is payable only after the measurement outcomes are confirmed through the validation process documented in the Project Schedule.

Commercial Structure

The commercial structure operationalises these principles through an integrated pricing arrangement:

This structure creates a partnership dynamic fundamentally different from traditional consulting. CBS Group has a direct financial interest in ensuring that initiatives succeed—not merely that deliverables are completed.

Value Categories

We focus on delivering measurable value across multiple dimensions:

Financial Benefits:

Direct cost reduction through process optimisation

Capital efficiency improvements

Lifecycle cost optimisation

Revenue enhancement opportunities where applicable

Operational Benefits:

Asset availability improvements

Maintenance optimisation

Performance enhancement

Risk reduction strategies

Strategic Benefits:

Innovation capability development

Knowledge transfer and capacity building

System integration improvements

Enhanced stakeholder satisfaction

3.4 Validation Framework

Our governance structure ensures transparent and rigorous benefit validation through agreed protocols, with streamlined processes for efficiency:

Self-Certification (Benefits < $100,000):

Documented methodology using pre-agreed templates

Supporting evidence and data

Contractor certification with appropriate liability

3 business day notification period

Expedited Peer Review (Benefits $100,000 - $250,000):

Streamlined TfNSW team validation

Pre-agreed calculation templates for common benefit types

5 business day review period

Joint certification process

Standard Peer Review (Benefits $250,000 - $500,000):

Independent TfNSW team validation

Agreed measurement protocols

10 business day review period

Joint certification process

Independent Validation (Benefits > $500,000):

Third-party auditor verification

Comprehensive documentation review

Formal validation report

20 business day review period

Pre-Agreed Value Templates:

At project commencement, the parties will agree standard calculation templates for common benefit categories including:

Maintenance cost reductions

Capital expenditure deferrals

Asset availability improvements

Process efficiency gains

Energy and sustainability savings

3.5 Initiative Selection and Formalisation Process

The process for progressing from opportunity identification to project commencement under Option A follows these steps:

Step 1: Opportunity Identification

Either party may identify a potential value-generating opportunity. CBSE will maintain an ongoing pipeline of opportunities through strategic analysis and engagement with TfNSW stakeholders.

Step 2: Joint Scoping

For each prioritised opportunity, CBSE and TfNSW will jointly:

Define the problem statement and anticipated outcome

Establish baseline costs or performance metrics

Agree the measurement methodology aligned with CATALYST 7™

Determine the number of teams and resource commitment required

Agree the start date, end date, and value measurement period

Negotiate the value share percentage and any applicable cap

Step 3: Project Schedule Execution

The agreed terms will be documented in a Project Schedule, which forms a binding appendix to the Master Agreement. Work on the initiative will not commence until both parties have executed the Project Schedule.

Step 4: Project Commencement

Upon execution of the Project Schedule:

Monthly retainers for the agreed number of teams commence

Baseline data is formally documented and certified

The project team is mobilised in accordance with the agreed composition

Step 5: Value Realisation and Payment

Upon completion of the initiative:

Value is measured in accordance with the agreed methodology

Benefits are validated through the applicable validation tier

Value share is calculated and paid as documented in the Project Schedule

Option B - Traditional engagement model

Traditional engagement pricing

The traditional engagement model follows standard industry practices, providing TfNSW with a familiar commercial structure for defined-scope projects.

Fee Structure:

Market-rate pricing per resource level

Fixed price or time and materials options available

Standard payment terms (30 days from invoice)

Variation management through formal change control procedures

Indicative Resource Rates:

Principal Consultant: $2,800 per day

Senior Consultant: $2,200 per day

Consultant: $1,800 per day

Analyst: $1,400 per day

Traditional Engagement Terms

Standard commercial terms aligned with NSW Government procurement guidelines:

Professional indemnity insurance: $20 million per occurrence

Public liability insurance: $20 million

12-month warranty on all deliverables

Clear acceptance criteria and review processes

Liability cap at 100% of fees paid, with standard exceptions

Combined engagement scenarios

The dual-pathway framework is designed to be adaptable, allowing for a combination of engagement models to suit the complexity and evolving needs of large-scale programs.

Hybrid Program Example

5.1 Hybrid Program Example

For a major asset management transformation program, a hybrid approach employs both engagement pathways with clear administrative separation:

Option A Components (via Project Schedules):

Strategy development and value identification phases

Innovation initiatives with measurable value potential

Each component governed by its own Project Schedule with agreed baseline, measurement methodology, and value share terms

Option B Components (via Work Orders):

Detailed design and technical implementation with defined scope

Technical investigations and feasibility studies

Each component governed by a traditional work order with fixed price or time and materials terms

Both pathways operate under the Master Framework Agreement, with Project Schedules and Work Orders forming binding appendices that document initiative-specific terms

Progressive Engagement Example

A progressive engagement model could be used to move from a traditional to a value-based model over time. A project could begin with a feasibility study conducted under Option B to define the scope and potential value. Based on the findings, the engagement could then transition to an Option A pilot project to test the value-based approach on a smaller scale. If successful, this could be scaled up to a full deployment under Option A.

Parallel Engagement Example

In situations where there are multiple, concurrent needs, the two options can be run in parallel. For example, Option A could be used for a tunnel operations project where the goal is to identify and implement cost savings, while Option B could be used for a bridge assessment that requires a standard technical review. This allows for the most appropriate commercial model to be applied to each individual stream of work.

Terms and Conditions - Proposed Contract Amendments

Enabling the Value-Based Model

The value-based engagement model requires certain amendments to standard contract terms. These amendments do not reduce TfNSW's protections—they enable the commercial structure necessary for shared risk and aligned returns.

Intellectual Property Protection

Balanced intellectual property provisions are essential to this framework. CBS Group's willingness to share value risk depends on protection of the methodologies and tools that enable us to deliver with confidence. We propose the following provisions:

Clause 4.1 - CBS Group IP Rights:

"All intellectual property rights in any materials, methodologies, frameworks, tools, or know-how that CBS Group brings to the engagement remain the exclusive property of CBS Group. TfNSW is granted no rights to use CBS Group's pre-existing IP except as specifically required for the purposes of the engagement under this agreement."

New Clause 12.7 - Partnership IP Management:

This clause establishes that CBS Group IP remains with CBS Group, ST Engineering IP remains with ST Engineering, TfNSW receives usage rights for NSW transport purposes, no transfer of IP occurs between parties without consent, and validation methodologies remain confidential.

To implement this innovative framework within TfNSW's standard Professional Services Contract, we propose the following specific amendments to accommodate both engagement pathways.

These amendments are designed to be applied to TfNSW's Professional Services Contract (Version 4.0, September 2024) as provided in Annexure A - Proposed Contract.

Proposed Contract amendment schedule

The following table outlines the specific amendments required to accommodate our dual-pathway framework:

Master framework agreement

Both engagement options will be governed by a comprehensive master framework agreement. This agreement will provide a consistent foundation for our partnership while allowing for the flexibility to accommodate the specific terms of each engagement option.

The framework agreement will establish the following key parameters:

Initial Term: A two-year initial term, with performance gates at 6, 12 and 18 months

Performance Gates: Clear metrics for value delivered, milestone achievement, and stakeholder satisfaction, with ability for either party to exit if minimum thresholds are not met

Extension Options: The agreement may be extended for two additional two-year terms, subject to achieving performance metrics and mutual agreement

Performance Reviews: Quarterly performance reviews against agreed key performance indicators

Option-Specific Schedules: Each engagement option will have its own schedule detailing specific terms

Governance and Reporting: A unified governance structure and reporting process will ensure consistent oversight

Governance and Partnership Management

Joint governance structure

The success of this framework relies on effective governance that promotes transparency, accountability, and continuous improvement. We propose a joint governance structure that ensures both parties have equal participation in strategic oversight and operational management.

Joint Steering Committee:

Equal representation from both TfNSW and CBSE (three members each)

Monthly meetings for strategic oversight and direction

Quarterly value assessment and benefit reviews

Annual partnership evaluation and strategic planning

Operational Management:

Dedicated relationship managers from both parties

Weekly operational meetings to address immediate needs

Real-time issue resolution protocols

Focus on continuous improvement and innovation

Value Validation Panel:

Independent chair for dispute resolution if required

Technical experts engaged as needed

Transparent validation processes with documented rationale

Agreed protocols for benefit measurement and verification

Implementation roadmap

Risk management approach

Both engagement options incorporate appropriate risk management strategies:

Shared Risks (Option A):

Value creation uncertainty is mitigated through our proven CATALYST 7™ methodology

Implementation dependencies are addressed through joint accountability mechanisms

Measurement complexity is managed through clear, agreed validation protocols

Traditional Risks (Option B):

Scope definition through detailed work orders and specifications

Quality assurance through defined acceptance criteria

Timeline management through milestone tracking and regular reporting

Intellectual Property Protection:

Clear delineation between CBSE proprietary methodologies and project deliverables

Appropriate confidentiality provisions and access controls

Agreed usage rights that protect both parties' interests

Why choose this alternative framework 

Key Framework Enhancements

This alternative framework incorporates several enhancements designed to address common concerns with innovative commercial models while maintaining the benefits of a value-based approach:

Refined Intellectual Property Framework
We have structured IP arrangements to protect CBS Group's proprietary methodologies while providing TfNSW with perpetual rights to use all project-specific applications and deliverables for internal purposes. This balances protection of our core IP with TfNSW's need for ongoing access to project outputs.

Performance-Based Contract Term
The framework features a 2-year initial term with clear performance gates at 6, 12, and 18 months, allowing both parties to assess progress and value delivery before committing to extensions. This provides security while maintaining flexibility.

Enhanced Retainer Value
The monthly retainer includes defined minimum deliverables such as performance dashboards, opportunity assessments, and strategic planning sessions, providing certainty of service levels regardless of value share outcomes.

Streamlined Validation Process
Pre-agreed templates and expedited review processes for benefits under $250,000 reduce administrative burden while maintaining rigorous validation standards. Common benefit types will have standardised calculation methodologies agreed upfront.

Balanced Termination Provisions
A sliding scale approach to termination payments (6 months Year 1, 4 months Year 2, 2 months thereafter) fairly recognises the diminishing investment recovery need over time while protecting CBSE's initial investment.

Innovation with options

This framework represents a significant advancement in procurement for government agencies, offering a unique combination of flexibility and innovation. The dual-pathway approach provides several strategic advantages:

It is one of the first dual-pathway frameworks to be offered in the Australian government sector, setting a new standard for flexible engagement models

It provides a choice between an innovative, value-based model and a traditional, time-based model, allowing TfNSW to align the commercial approach with the risk appetite of each project

It offers the flexibility to match the engagement model to the specific needs of each project, with the ability to use different models for different projects concurrently

It enables risk-sharing on transformational initiatives, fostering a genuine partnership, while retaining traditional options for more defined scopes of work

A proven partnership

CBSE brings together two organisations with complementary strengths and proven track records of success:

CBS Group:

Has a 20-year history of serving TfNSW, providing deep understanding of its assets, processes, and stakeholders

Has delivered over $1 billion in validated savings through innovative approaches

Brings proprietary CATALYST 7™ methodology with demonstrated results

Egis:

Offers global expertise and commitment to innovation

Brings 18,000 professionals delivering major projects worldwide

Provides international best practices and technical depth

Our combined credentials, which include successful engagements on projects such as the Western Harbour Tunnel, the Sydney Harbour Tunnel, and the M6 Stage 1, demonstrate our capability and commitment to delivering value.

Next steps

Proposed way forward

We welcome the opportunity to discuss this alternative framework with TfNSW and work together to refine it to meet your specific needs. We suggest the following immediate steps:

Anticipated Outcomes

Through this partnership framework, we anticipate achieving:

Director, 
CBSE JV

This proposal represents CBSE's commitment to developing a collaborative partnership with Transport for NSW that delivers measurable value through innovative commercial models. We look forward to working together to implement a framework that aligns our mutual interests and delivers enhanced outcomes for the communities we serve.

| 10.4 Termination for convenience | Standard termination provisions | Value-based model requires appropriate consideration of investment made by Contractor | Add: "For value-based engagement terminations for convenience: Principal pays pro-rata share of validated benefits achieved to termination date plus investment recovery payment based on sliding scale: 6 months' retainer (if terminated in Year 1), 4 months' retainer (if terminated in Year 2), 2 months' retainer (if terminated after Year 2). This recognises the diminishing investment recovery need over time." |
